question:
Solute ‘${\text{X}}$’ dimerises in water to the extent of $80\% ,\,\,2.5\,{\text{g}}$ of ‘${\text{X}}$’ in 100 g of water increases the boiling point by $0.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The molar mass of ‘${\text{X}}$’ is
$\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation:
Given: ${{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}} = 2.5;{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}} = 100\,{\text{g}},$
${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = 0.52\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}},\,\,\,\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} = 0.3^\circ {\text{C}}$
van't Hoff factor,
${\text{i}} = 1 + \alpha \left( {\frac{1}{{\text{n}}} - 1} \right)$
${\text{i}} = 1 + 0.8\left( {\frac{1}{2} - 1} \right)$
${\text{i}} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$
${\text{As }}\quad \Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} = {\text{i}}\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\text{m}}$
$ = {\text{i}} \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \times \frac{{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}} \times 1000}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}} \times {{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}}}$
$0.3 = 0.6 \times 0.52 \times \frac{{2.5}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{100}}$
${{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}} = \frac{{0.6 \times 0.52 \times 2.5 \times 1000}}{{0.3 \times 100}} = 26\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}$

question: Match the following concepts related to abnormal molar mass with their correct descriptions. Choose the correct options from the codes given below:
explanation:
A - When ionic compounds dissociate in solution, they increase the number of particles, leading to a lower experimental molar mass than the true molar mass .
B - When molecules like ethanoic acid associate in a solution, it decreases the number of particles, resulting in a higher experimental molar mass .
C - The van't Hoff factor (i) is the ratio of the normal molar mass to the abnormal molar mass and accounts for the change due to association or dissociation .
D - Abnormal molar mass is the molar mass determined experimentally which may be affected by the dissociation or association of solute in the solution. Correct option is (1)

The molar mass of KCl calculated from the boiling point elevation in water would be $37.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ if it completely dissociates.
The experimental molar mass is half of the actual molar mass if there is complete dissociation of KCl .
explanation: If KCl completely dissociates, it will release twice the number of moles of particles compared to the undissociated state. question: pH of a 0.1 M monobasic acid is 2 . Its osmotic pressure at a given temperature $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ is (Given that the effective concentration for osmotic pressure is $(1+\alpha) \times$ concentration of acid $\alpha$ is the dissociation factor
explanation:
$\mathrm{pH}=2$
$\mathrm{C}=0.1=\mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{i}=1+\mathrm{x}=1+0.1=1.1$
Using formula,
$\pi=\mathrm{iCRT}=1.1 \times 0.1 \mathrm{RT}=0.11 \mathrm{RT}$

question: If 0.1 M solution of NaCl is isotonic with $1.1 \dfrac{\mathrm{~W}}{\mathrm{~V}} \%$ urea solution, the degree of ionisation of NaCl is (molar masses of urea and NaCl are 60 and $58.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
explanation:
Isotonic means $\pi_{\mathrm{NaCl}}=\pi_{\text {urea }}$
${{\text{i}}_{{\text{NaCl}}}} \times {{\text{M}}_{{\text{NaCl}}}} = {{\text{M}}_{{\text{urea }}}}\quad ({\text{i}} = 1$ for Urea)
$0.1 \times \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{NaCl}}=\dfrac{1.1}{60} \times \dfrac{1000}{100}$
$\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{NaCl}}=1.83$
Degree of dissociation, $\alpha = \frac{{{\text{i}} - 1}}{{{\text{n}} - 1}} = \frac{{1.83 - 1}}{{2 - 1}}$
( $\mathrm{n}=2$ for NaCl as it produces 2 ions)
$\alpha=0.83$

question: At 300 K , the osmotic pressure of a decinormal solution of sodium chloride is 4.82 atm.  The  degree of dissociation of sodium chloride is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2}$. The value of x is  $({\text{R}} = 0.082\,{\text{L}}\,{\text{atm}}\,{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
$\pi=\mathrm{iCRT}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{i}=\dfrac{\pi}{\mathrm{CRT}}=\dfrac{4.82}{01 \times 0.082 \times 300}=1.96$
$\alpha=\dfrac{\mathrm{i}-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}=\dfrac{1.96-1}{2-1}=0.96$
$\% \alpha=96 \%$

question: The freezing point of equimolal aqueous solution will be highest for
explanation:
Freezing point $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { van't Hoff factor (i) }}$
For $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{HNH}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{i}=2$
For $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}, \mathrm{i}=3$
For $\mathrm{LaCl}_{3}, \mathrm{i}=4$; For $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}, \mathrm{i}=1$
So, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ has minimum value of van't Hoff factor, hence, its freezing point will be highest.

$\therefore$ Order of colligative properties :
$\mathrm{MgCl}_{2}=\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}>\mathrm{NaCl}$.

question: The freezing point depression of 0.001 m of $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{y}}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$ is $5.58 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~K}$. If the oxidation state of Fe is +2 and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{k}_{.} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$, then the total number of possibilities for different types of A and B cations are
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \cdot \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
$5.58 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{i} \times 1.86 \times 0.001$
$\mathrm{i}=\dfrac{5.58 \times 10^{-3}}{1.86 \times 0.001}=3$
So, one A & one B should present to get total 3 ions. $\mathrm{Fe}^{+2}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$, so total charge of $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{~B}$ is +4 .
$\therefore$ Possibilities are $\mathrm{A}^{+}, \mathrm{B}^{+3} ; \mathrm{A}^{+2}, \mathrm{~B}^{+2} ; \mathrm{A}^{+3}, \mathrm{~B}^{+}$
subject: Chemistry

question: When 2.44 grams of benzoic acid ($\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}$) dissolved in 25 grams of benzene, it shows depression of freezing point equal to 2.2 K .   Molal depression constant of benzene is $5.0 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. What is the percentage association of acid, if it forms dimer in solution?
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \cdot \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
$2.2=\mathrm{i} \times 5 \times \dfrac{2.44}{122} \times \dfrac{1000}{25} \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}=0.55$
$\alpha = \frac{{1 - {\text{i}}}}{{1 - \frac{1}{{\text{n}}}}}$
$({\text{n}} = 2$ as it undergoes dimerization)
$\alpha=\dfrac{1-0.55}{1-\dfrac{1}{n}}=\dfrac{0.45}{0.5}=0.9$
$\% \alpha=90 \%$

question:
Correct order of osmotic pressure of the following solutions is
(i) $30 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of glucose
(ii) $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$
(iii) $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of glucose
(iv) $58.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ of NaCl
explanation:
$\pi \alpha \mathrm{i} \cdot \mathrm{C}$
(i) Glucose, $\mathrm{i}=1, \mathrm{C}=0.166 \mathrm{M}$
(ii) Urea, ${\text{i}} = 1,{\text{C}} = 1\,{\text{M}}$
(iii) Glucose, $\mathrm{i}=1, \mathrm{C}=0.444 \mathrm{M}$
(iv) $\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{i}=2, \mathrm{C}=1 \mathrm{M}$.
So, correct order is iv $ < $ ii $ < $ iii $ < $ i

question: The osmotic pressure of 0.1 M monobasic acid of pH 3 at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{pH}=3 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-\mathrm{pH}}=10^{-3}$
$\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{C} \cdot \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{0.001}{0.1}=0.01$
$\mathrm{i}=1+\alpha=1+0.01=1.01$
$\because$ Osmotic pressure $(\pi)=\mathrm{iCRT}$
$\pi=1.01 \times 0.1 \times 0.08 \times 300=2.42 \mathrm{~atm}$
Hence, (1) is the correct answer.

question: A $1.17 \%$ solution of solute $A$ is isotonic with $7.2 \%$ solution of glucose. If the molecular weight of solute A is 58.5, the value of van't Hoff factor, ' i '  is
explanation:
For isotonic solutions,
$\mathrm{C}_{\text {Glucose }}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}} \cdot \mathrm{i}$
$\dfrac{7.2}{180} \times \dfrac{1000}{100}=\dfrac{1.17}{58.5} \times \dfrac{1000}{100} \times \mathrm{i}$
${\text{i}} = \frac{{7.2 \times 58.5}}{{180 \times 1.17}} = 2$

question: The observed molar mass determined for $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ by freezing point depression method  is $50.0 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. The molecular weight of   $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is 142 . What will be the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ for $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in water?
explanation:
van't Hoff factor, ${\text{i}} = \frac{{{{({\text{ Molar mass }})}_{{\text{calculated }}}}}}{{{{({\text{ Molar mass }})}_{{\text{observed }}}}}}$ $=\dfrac{142}{50}=2.84$
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons \underbrace{2 \mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}_{\begin{array}{c}\text { Number of } \\ \text { ions, } \mathrm{n}=3\end{array}}$
$\therefore \quad \alpha = \frac{{{\text{i}} - 1}}{{{\text{n}} - 1}} = \frac{{2.84 - 1}}{{3 - 1}} = 0.92$

question: The solubility product of a sparingly soluble  ${\text{A}}{{\text{B}}_2}$  salt is $2.56 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}^{3}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of water is $1.8 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The depression in freezing point of a standard solution of $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ is
explanation:
Let, solubility of $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$ (1:2 type electrolyte) is pure water $ = {\text{S}}\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{{\text{L}}^{ - 1}} = {\text{SM}}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 \mathrm{~S}^{3}=2.56 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}^{3}$ (given)
$\therefore \mathrm{S}=0.04 \mathrm{M}=0.04 \mathrm{~m}$ (molal) under standard condition of the solution.
Depression of freezing point,
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{i}=1.8 \times 0.04 \times 3=0.216 \mathrm{~K}$
$\left[\because\right.$ Assuming complete dissociation of $\mathrm{AB}_{2}$, van't Hoff factor, $\mathrm{i}=3$ ]

question:
Calculate the approximate $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ (in K ) for 0.001 molal KCl solution, if its van' t -Hoff factor is 1.98
[$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{m}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.98 \times 0.52 \times 0.001=0.0010296$
$\therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.03 \times 10^{-3}$

question: If the degree of association is $70 \%$ for the reaction $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons(\mathrm{~A})_{2}$ the van't Hoff factor for the solute A is
explanation:
Given, $\alpha=0.7, \mathrm{n}=2$
$\mathrm{i}=1+\alpha\left(\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{n}}-1\right)$
$=1+0.7\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-1\right)$ $=0.65$

question: Which of the following aqueous solution will exhibit highest boiling point?
explanation:
$\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}} = {\rm{i}}\,{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}} \times {\rm{m}}$
$\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}} \propto {\rm{i}} \times {\rm{m}}$
By considering molarity same as molality
(1) $0.015 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6} \quad \mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m}=1 \times 0.015=0.015$
(2) $0.01\,{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{Urea }}\quad {\text{i}} \times {\text{m}} = 1 \times 0.01 = 0.01$
(3) $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KNO}_{3} \quad \mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m}=2 \times 0.01=0.02$
(4) $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \quad \mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m}=3 \times 0.01=0.03$
Higher the value of $(\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m})$ more will be the boiling point.

question:
Arrange the following solutions in order of their decreasing boiling points.
(I) ${10^{ - 4}}{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{NaCl}}$
(II) ${10^{ - 3}}{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{NaCl}}$
(III) ${10^{ - 2}}{\text{M}}\,\,{\text{NaCl}}$
(IV) $10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ urea
explanation:
Higher the elevation in boiling point, higher will be the boiling point.
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}} \propto \mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m}$
For urea, $\mathrm{i}=1$
For $\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{i}=2$
$\therefore$ Boiling point order: III > II > I > IV.

question: The solution from the following with highest depression in freezing point/lowest freezing point is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\Delta T_{f}=i \times K_{f} \times m}}$ or ${\mathrm{\Delta T_{f} \propto i m}}$ or
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \propto \mathrm{i} \dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{\mathrm{M}}}}$
Where, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{w}=}}$ Mass of compound and, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{M}=}}$ Molar mass of compound
For acetic acid in benzene, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{i} < 1}}$ (association takes place)
For benzoic acid in benzene, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{i} < 1}}$ (association takes place)
For glucose in water, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{i}=1}}$
For acetic acid in water, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{i}>1}}$ (diassociation takes place)
Molecular mass of acetic acid ${\mathrm{=60}}$ g. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-}}}$
Molecular mass of glucose ${\mathrm{=180 \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-}}}$
Molecular mass of benzoic acid ${\mathrm{=122 \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{\mathrm{m}}}}$ value is highest for acetic acid.
Hence, 180 g of accetic acid dissolved in water will have highest depression in freezing point or lowest freezing point.So, the correct option is (4).

options.text: 180 g of acetic acid dissolved in benzene
180 g of benzoic acid dissolved in benzene
180 g of glucose dissolved in water
180 g of acetic acid dissolved in water.

question: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{HgI}_{4}}}$ is ${\mathrm{40 \%}}$ ionised in its aqueous solution. The value of its van't Hoff factor is ____ .
explanation:
${\mathrm{\alpha=\dfrac{i-1}{n^{\prime}-1}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Hgl}_{4} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\left[\mathrm{HgI}_{4}\right]^{2-}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \mathrm{n}^{\prime}=3}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 0.40=\dfrac{\mathrm{i}-1}{3-1}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{i}=(0.40 \times 2)+1=1.8}}$

question:
Assertion: Molar mass of acetic acid found by the depression of freezing point method, separately in the solvents water and benzene are different.

Reason: Water helps in ionization but benzene brings association of acetic acid. Identify the correct option.
explanation:
Acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ exists as dimer in benzene solvent due to hydrogen bonding. As the colligative properties are inversely proportional to the molar mass of the solute so in this case molar mass will be greater than theoretical value.
But in case of water, acetic acid dissociates (very less dissociation).
$\begin{gathered} 2{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}}\mathop \rightleftharpoons \limits^{{\text{ Benzene }}} {\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}}} \right)_2} \hfill \\ {{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}}\mathop \rightleftharpoons \limits^{{\text{ Water }}} {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^ - } + {{\text{H}}^ + } \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
So, in case of benzene as solvent the molar mass of acetic acid will be nearly double (120) than their normal value (60).

options.text: Both Assertion  and Reason are correct ;Reason is the correct explanation for Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct ;but Reason is not the correct explanation for Assertion
Assertion is true  but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

question: Pure benzene freezes at $5.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. A solution of $0.223 \mathrm{~g}$ of phenylacetic acid $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}\right)$ in $4.4 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene $\left( {{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{f}}} = {\rm{5}}{\rm{.12}}\,{\rm{K}}\,{\rm{kg}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}} \right){\rm{freezes}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{at}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4}}{\rm{.47^\circ C}}$. From the observation, one can conclude that
explanation:
Depression in freezing point,
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{m}}$ (where, m = molality)
$ = 5.12 \times \frac{{0.223 \times 1000}}{{4.4 \times 136}}$
$ = 5.12 \times 0.372 = 1.91^\circ {\rm{C}}$
$\therefore$ Theoretical ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = 1.91$
Actual ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = 5.3^\circ {\rm{C}} - 4.47^\circ {\rm{C}}$
$ = 0.83^\circ {\rm{C}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{i}} = 0.83/1.91 = 0.43$
$\because {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{i}} < 1$
$\therefore$ Association will take place.Thus, it can be said that phenylacetic acid dimerises in benzene.

options.text: phenylacetic acid exists as such in benzene
phenylacetic acid undergoes partial ionisation in benzene
phenylacetic acid undergoes complete ionisation in benzene

question: $1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\rm{m}}$ solution of  ${\rm{Pt}}{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}} \right)_4}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_4}$ in ${{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}}$  shows depression in freezing point by $0.0054^\circ {\rm{C}}$. explanation:
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = {\rm{i}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{m}} = 0.0054$
$ = {\rm{i}} \times 1.86 \times 0.001$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{i}} = \frac{{5.4}}{{1.86}} = 3$
So, structure of compound is
$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
Since, it gives 3 moles on dissociation.$\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{+2}=1$ and $2 \,\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{NaCl}$ in water and organic acids in benzene show abnormal molecular mass.


Reason: Abnormal molecular mass is obtained when the substance in the solution undergoes dissociation or association.
explanation: Dissociation increases the number of particles, raising colligative property values, and decreases effective molecular mass. Similarly association decreases particle number, lowering colligative property values and increases effective molecular mass. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion: Lowering of vapour pressure is directly proportional to the number of ions present in the solution.


Reason: The vapour pressure of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ sugar solution is less than that of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ potassium chloride solution.
explanation:
The greater the Van't Hoff factor (i) greater is the relative lowering of vapor pressure.
For $\mathrm{KCl}$, ' $\mathrm{i}$ ' value is 2 as it is an ionic compound of $\mathrm{K}+$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. For sugar, "i" value is 1 as it is not an electrolyte. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

${\text{3}}{{\text{0}}_{{\text{nB}}}}{\text{ + 120 + 15}}{{\text{0}}_{{\text{nB}}}}$

question: The freezing point depression constant for water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If ${\text{5}}{\text{.00\,g  N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is dissolved in $45.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, the freezing point is changed by $-3.82^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.Calculate the van't Hoff factor for $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ :
explanation:
using; $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{i}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}} \times 1000}$
$=\dfrac{3.82 \times 45}{1.86 \times \dfrac{5}{142} \times 1000}=2.63$

question: $\mathrm{KBr}$ is $80 \%$ dissociated in aqueous solution of 0.5 m concentration (given, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water ${\text{ = 1}}{\text{.86}}\,{\text{K}}\,\,{\text{Kg }}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}$ ). The solution freezes at:
explanation:
Here degree of dissociation $=80 \%$ or 0.8
$\alpha = \frac{{{\text{i - 1}}}}{{{\text{n - 1}}}}$
${\text{0}}{\text{.8 = }}\frac{{{\text{i - 1}}}}{{{\text{2 - 1}}}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{i}=1.8$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}=1.8 \times 1.86 \times 0.5 \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.674$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{0}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow 1.674=273-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=271.326 \mathrm{~K}$

question: In a 0.2 molal aqueous solution of a weak acid $\mathrm{HX}$, the degree of ionization is 0.3 . Taking $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for water as 1.85 , the freezing point of the solution will be nearest to
explanation:
$\alpha = \frac{{{\text{i - 1}}}}{{{\text{n - 1}}}}$
$0.3 = \frac{{{\text{i - 1}}}}{{{\text{2 - 1}}}}$
$\mathrm{i}=1.3$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{ik}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}$
$=1.3 \times 1.85 \times 0.2=0.48$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$ $=0-0.48$
$=-0.48^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

question: Which among the following colligative properties is useful to determine molar masses of very expensive solutes?
explanation: As compared to other colligative properties, the magnitude of osmotic pressure is large even for very dilute solutions. The osmotic pressure can be measured at a suitable constant temperature and the molar masses can be measured far more accurately. Therefore, osmotic pressure is more useful to determine molar masses of very expensive solutes.

question: $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ solutions of $\mathrm{KCl}$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ are prepared in water. The freezing point of $\mathrm{KCl}$ is found to be $-2{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the freezing point of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ to be completely ionised?
explanation:
$\mathrm{i}$ ' for $\mathrm{KCl}=2,\left(\mathrm{KCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)$
' $\mathrm{i}$ ' for $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}=3\left(\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ba}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)$
$\because \Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} \propto {\text{i }}$
$\frac{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}({\text{KCl}})}}{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}\left( {{\text{BaC}}{{\text{l}}_2}} \right)}} = \frac{2}{3}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}\left( {{\text{BaC}}{{\text{l}}_2}} \right) = \frac{3}{2} \times 2 = 3^\circ {\text{C}}$
$\therefore$ Freezing point of $\mathrm{KCl}$
$=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\alpha = }}\frac{{{\rm{i - 1}}}}{{\left( {{\rm{a + b - 1}}} \right)}}$

${\rm{\% \alpha = 83}}{\rm{.96\% }}$

question: If the various terms in the given below expressions have usual meanings, the van’t Hoff factor (i) cannot be calculated by which one of the following expressions?
explanation:
Van’t Hoff equation is
${\rm{\pi V = inRT}}$
For depression in freezing point,
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = i \times }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ \times m}}$
For elevation in boiling point,
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = i \times }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ \times m}}$
For lowering of vapour pressure,
$\frac{{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{solvent}}}^{\rm{0}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{solution}}}}}}{{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{solvent}}}^{\rm{0}}}}{\rm{ = i}}\left( {\frac{{\rm{n}}}{{{\rm{N + n}}}}} \right){\rm{.}}$

question: A solute ‘S’ undergoes a reversible trimerization when dissolved in a certain solvent. The boiling point elevation of its 0.1 molal solution was found to be identical to the boiling point elevation in case of a 0.08 molal solution of a solute which neither undergoes association nor dissociation. To what percent had the solute ‘S’ undergone trimerization?
explanation:
${\text{3S}}{\mkern 1mu} \rightleftharpoons {\mkern 1mu} {{\text{S}}_{\text{3}}}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}$
${\rm{1 - \alpha }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{\rm{\alpha }}}{{\rm{3}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{i = 1 - }}\frac{{{\rm{2\alpha }}}}{{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{Now}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}\left( {{\rm{1 - }}\frac{{{\rm{2\alpha }}}}{{\rm{3}}}} \right){\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.08}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\alpha = 0}}{\rm{.3}}{\rm{.}}$ Hence, 30% trimerization.

question:
The molecular weight of acetic acid determined by depression in freezing point method in benzene and water was found to be different.
Water is polar and benzene is non-polar.
explanation:
In benzene, acetic acid dimerises but in water it does not. This is because benzene is non-polar, whereas water is polar. The number of particles become different in both the solvents.
Depression in freezing point is a colligative property, i.e. depends upon the number of particles in solution. Hence, molecular mass determined by depression in freezing point method comes out to be different.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor
subtopic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor
parent_topic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor

question:
Calculate Van’t Hoff-factor for 0.2 m aqueous solution of KCl which freezes at ${\rm{ - 0}}{\rm{.68}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$.
$\left( {{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.86K}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given, Molality $ = 0.2\,\,m$
${K_f} = 1.86K\,\,mo{l^{ - }}$
$\Delta {T_f} = 0.680^\circ C = 0.680K$
As, we know that
$\Delta {T_f} = i \times {K_f} \times m$
$0.680 = i \times 1.86 \times 0.2$
$\frac{{0.680}}{{1.86 \times 0.2}} = i$
$1.83 = i$

question: If a centimolal aqueous solution of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]$ has degree of dissociation 0.78. What is the value of van’t Hoff factor?
explanation:
Given,
Degree of dissociation $\left( \alpha \right) = 0.78$
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right] \to {\rm{3}}{{\rm{K}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + }}{\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]^{{\rm{3 - }}}}$
That means, total moles formed after dissociation
$\left( {\rm{n}} \right){\rm{ = 4}}$
We know that, van’t Hoff factor (i) ${\rm{ = \alpha n + }}\left( {{\rm{1 - \alpha }}} \right)$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.78 \times 4 + }}\left( {{\rm{1 - 0}}{\rm{.78}}} \right)$
${\rm{ = 3}}{\rm{.122 + 0}}{\rm{.22 = 3}}{\rm{.34}}$
subject: Chemistry
topic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor
subtopic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor
parent_topic: Abnormal Molar Masses and Van't Hoff Factor

question: 2 g of benzoic acid dissolved in 25 g ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{6}}}$ shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{for}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{6}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{is}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{4}}{\rm{.9}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{K}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{molalit}}{{\rm{y}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$. If acid forms dimer molecule in solution, then the percentage association of acid is
explanation:
${\rm{\Delta T = }}\frac{{{\rm{1000 \times }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ \times w}}}}{{{\rm{m \times W}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1000 \times 4}}{\rm{.9 \times 2}}}}{{{\rm{122 \times 25}}}}{\rm{ = 3}}{\rm{.213K}}$
${\rm{i = }}\frac{{{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{{\rm{exp}}{\rm{.}}}}}}{{{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{{\rm{cal}}}}}}{\rm{ = 1 - \alpha + }}\frac{{\rm{\alpha }}}{{\rm{n}}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.62}}}}{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.12}}}}{\rm{ = 1 - \alpha + }}\frac{{\rm{\alpha }}}{{\rm{2}}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\alpha = 0}}{\rm{.992}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{99}}{\rm{.2\% }}$

question: Van’t Hoff factor of centimolal solution of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]$ is 3.333. Calculate the percent dissociation of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]$.
explanation:
Given, i = 3.333
On dissociation of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]$, we get four ions.
${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{3}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right] \to {\rm{3}}{{\rm{K}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ + }}{\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{\left( {{\rm{CN}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]^{{\rm{3 - }}}}$
Total number of ions (n) = 4
$\therefore {\rm{\alpha = }}\frac{{{\rm{i - 1}}}}{{{\rm{n - 1}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3}}{\rm{.333 - 1}}}}{{{\rm{4 - 1}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2}}{\rm{.333}}}}{{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.7776}}$
$ \simeq {\rm{0}}{\rm{.78}}$
Hence, the per cent dissociation becomes ${\rm{78\% }}$

question:
A 0.5 molal aqueous solution of a weak acid (HX) is 20 percent ionised. The lowering in freezing point of this solution is
$\left( {{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.86}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{K/m}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{for}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{water}}} \right)$
explanation:
${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = i}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{m}}$
We know, ${\rm{\alpha = }}\frac{{{\rm{i - 1}}}}{{{\rm{n - 1}}}}$ (dissociation)
${\rm{\alpha = }}\frac{{{\rm{i - 1}}}}{{{\rm{2 - 1}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{i = \alpha + 1}}$
${\rm{so,\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = }}\left( {{\rm{\alpha + 1}}} \right){{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{m}}$
${\rm{Given,\alpha = 0}}{\rm{.2,molality = 0}}{\rm{.5,K}}_{\rm{f}}^{\rm{ = }}{\rm{1}}{\rm{.86}}$
$\therefore {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{F}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.5 \times 1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{\rm{.86 = 1}}{\rm{.116K}}$

question: If the van’t Hoff-factor for 0.1 M Ba ${\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}$ solution is 2.74, the degree of dissociation is
explanation:
Given,
Molarity = 0.1 M
vant Hoff factor (i) = 2.74
Since, ${\rm{i > 1}}$, it means solute is undergoing dissociation.
$Ba{\left( {N{O_3}} \right)_2} \rightleftharpoons B{A^{2 + }} + 2NO_3^ - $
Number of particles dissociated (n) = 3
Now, ${\rm{\alpha }}$ (degree of dissociation) ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( {{\rm{i - 1}}} \right)}}{{\left( {{\rm{n - 1}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( {{\rm{2}}{\rm{.74 - 1}}} \right)}}{{\left( {{\rm{3 - 1}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.87}}$

question: Which of the following is not a colligative property?
explanation: Colligative property depends on the concentration of solution and not on the type of particles, while optical activity depends on the type of particles.
subject: Chemistry

question:
After adding non-volatile solute freezing point of water decreases to $-0.186^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}$ if ${{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = 1.86\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$ and
${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = 0.521\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
explanation:
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times {\text{m}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{m}} = \frac{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}}} = \frac{{0.186}}{{1.86}} = 0.1\,{\text{m}}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \times {\text{m}} = 0.521 \times 0.1 = 0.0521\,{\text{K}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: On mixing urea, the boiling point of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is changed to $100.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Calculate the freezing point of the solution, if   $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of water is $1.87 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
explanation:
$\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.5}{\Delta \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{0.52}{1.87}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.8$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-1.8=-1.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: The total number of colligative properties are
explanation: Total colligative properties are four.

question: Match column I with column II.
explanation:
Relative lowering of vapour pressure, $\frac{{{\rm{P}}^\circ - {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{\rm{P}}^\circ }} = {\rm{i}}.{{\rm{X}}_{{\rm{solute }}}}$
Elevation in boiling point, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\circ}=\mathrm{i} . \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
Depression in freezing point, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} . \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
Osmotic pressure, $\pi=$ iCRT .

question:
Assertion: Elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point are colligative properties.


Reason: All colligative properties are used for the calculation of molecular masses.
explanation: Colligative properties like elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point, dependent on solute particles, and all colligative Properties are used to measure molar mass. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

subject: Chemistry

question: The colligative property is not represented by
explanation:
Colligative properties are defined as properties of dilute solutions of non-volatile solutes whose values depend upon the number of solute particles in the solution but not on the individual identity of the solute. Various colligative properties are :
(1) Relative lowering of vapour pressure
(2) Elevation in boiling point
(3) Depression in freezing point
(4) Osmotic pressure
and optical activity is not a colligative property.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following physical properties is used to determine, the molecular mass of a polymer solution?
explanation: Best method to determine the molecular mass of colligative property is osmotic pressure
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is not a colligative property?
explanation: Relative lowering of vapour pressure is a colligative property. As, it depends only upon the no. of moles of the solute and solvent and is independent of nature of the solute.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The presence of a non-volatile solute in a solution decreases the freezing point of the solvent.
The freezing point depression is a colligative property, meaning it depends only on the quantity and not the identity of the solute particles.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because the addition of a non-volatile solute lowers the freezing point of solvent known as freezing point depression. Statement B is true because freezing point depression is indeed a colligative property, which depends on the number of solute particles present and not their chemical identity. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

Depression of the freezing point of a solvent increases with the addition of a solute.
The depression of the freezing point is used to measure the molar mass of macromolecules in solution.
explanation: The freezing point depression is another colligative property that is affected by the presence of a solute and is indeed used for determining the molar masses of  macromolecules.  Correct option is (1)

Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The depression of the freezing point is a colligative property that can be used to determine the molar mass of solute.

Reason: The molar mass of a solute can be calculated from the molality of the solution and the degree of the freezing point depression using the cryoscopic constant of the solvent.
explanation: The depression of the freezing point is indeed a colligative property, and it is used along with the cryoscopic constant to calculate the molar mass of solute. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.

subject: Chemistry

question: The freezing point of solution containing 10 mL of non-volatile and non-electrolyte liquid " A " in 500 g of water is $-0.413^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of water is $1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the molecular weight of $\mathrm{A}=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, what is the density of the solution in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}$ ? (Assume $\Delta_{\text {mix }} \mathrm{V}=0$)
explanation:
Given, Volume of solute $=10 \mathrm{~mL}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-(-0.413)=0.413^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}$
$0.413=1.86 \times \dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{60} \times \dfrac{1000}{500}$
$W_{B}=\dfrac{0.413 \times 60 \times 500}{1.86 \times 1000}=6.66 \mathrm{~g}$
$\because$ Density (d) of solution $=\dfrac{\text { Total mass }}{\text { Total volume }}$
$\mathrm{d}=\dfrac{6.66+500}{500+10}=\dfrac{506.66}{510}=0.993 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question:
An aqueous solution of a non-volatile solute boils at $100.17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature at which this solution will freeze (in $^\circ {\text{C}})$ is
$\left( {{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right) = 0.512^\circ {\text{C}}\,\,{\text{kg}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}} \right.,$ $\left. {{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right) = 1.86^\circ {\text{C}}\,\,{\text{kg}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}} \right)$
explanation:
We know that, $\frac{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}}}{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}}}$
$\dfrac{0.17}{\Delta \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{0.512}{1.86} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0.62$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-0.62=-0.62^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A solvent freezes at $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and its latent heat of fusion is $180 \mathrm{Jg}^{-1}$. The molal depression constant of the solvent is [units of  ${{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = {\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}]$
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=$ ? $\mathrm{T}=17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=17+273=290 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{f}}^{2}}{1000 \times \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{8.314 \times(290)^{2}}{1000 \times 180}$
$=3.88 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following columns.
explanation:
A - R; Molal elevation constant or ebullioscopic constant is defined as the elevation in boiling point when one mole of non-volatile solute is added to one kilogram of solvent. Its unit is $\mathrm{K} \mathrm{kgmol}{ }^{-1}$.
B - P ; Cryoscopic constant is defined as the depression of freezing point of the solvent produced on dissolving one mole of a substance in 1000 g of it. It is also called molal depression constant.
C - S; Henry's law is a gas law which states that the amount of gas that is dissolved in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas above the liquid when the temperature is kept constant.
D - Q; Dalton's law states that in a mixture of non-reacting gases, the total pressure exerted is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases.
subject: Chemistry

question: The freezing point of an aqueous solution containing 25 g of ethanol in 1000 g of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is $\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation:
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times \frac{{{{\text{w}}_{\text{B}}} \times 1000}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}} \times {{\text{w}}_{\text{A}}}}}$
$=1.86 \times \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{46 \times 1000}=1.01$
$\therefore$ Freezing point of solution
$=0^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-1.01=-1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Calculate the molal depression constant of a solution, which freezes at $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The latent heat of fusion is $180.7 \mathrm{Jg}^{-1}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{f}}^{2}}{1000 \times \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{8.314 \times(288)^{2}}{1000 \times 180.7}$
$=3.81 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{al}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A camphor sample melts at $176^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for camphor is  $40\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}.$  A solution of 0.02 g of a hydrocarbon in 0.8 g of camphor melts at $156.77^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The hydrocarbon is made up of $92.3 \%$ of carbon. What is the molecular formula of the hydrocarbon?
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=176-156.77=19.2$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1000}{\text { mass of solvent }}$
$19.2=40 \times \dfrac{0.02}{\text { molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1000}{0.8}$
$\therefore$ Molar mass $=52.1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Element}}}&{{\text{mass}}\,{\text{% }}}&{{\text{no}}{\text{.of}}\,{\text{moles}}}&{{\text{ratio}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{moles}}} \\ {\text{C}}&{92.3}&{\frac{{92.3}}{{12}} = 7.7}&1 \\ {\text{H}}&{7.7}&{\frac{{7.7}}{1} = 7.7}&1 \end{array}$
So, empirical formula is CH
E.F. mass $=12+1=13$
$\mathrm{n}=\dfrac{\text { Molar mass }}{\text { E.f.mass }}=\dfrac{52}{13}=4$
Molecular formula $=(\mathrm{CH})_{4}=\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{4}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 2.0 g of a non-electrolyte dissolved in 100 g of benzene lowers the freezing point of benzene by 1.2 K . The freezing point depression constant of benzene is  $5.12\,{\text{K}}\,\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}.$  The molar mass of the solute is
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{1}}$
$1 \cdot 2=\dfrac{5 \cdot 12 \times 2 \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times 100}$
${{\text{M}}_2} = 85\,{\text{g}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What happens to freezing point of benzene when small quantity of naphthalene is added to benzene?
explanation: When a non-volatile solute is added, freezing point of solution decreases. So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry

question: One molal aqueous solution of urea freezes at $-1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Aqueous solution of ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{n}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{2n}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{n}}}$  $36\% $ by mass of water freezes at $-3.72^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the value of n ?
explanation:
Molality of urea solution $=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}({\text{urea}}) = 0 - \left( { - 1.86^\circ } \right) = 1.86^\circ $
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = \left( {\frac{{1000\,{{\text{W}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{1}}}}}} \right){{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = {\text{molality}}\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}$
$\therefore 1.86^\circ = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times 1\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{\text{kg}}$
$\therefore {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = 186^\circ {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{\text{kg}}$
${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2n}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{n}}} = 36{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{in}}\,\,100\,\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = \left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2n}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{n}}}} \right) = \frac{{1000\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{1}}}}}$
$\therefore {{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2n}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{n}}}} \right) = \frac{{1000\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{1}}}}}$
$ = \frac{{1000 \times 1.86 \times 36}}{{3.72 \times 100}}$
${{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2n}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{n}}}} \right) = 180\,{\text{g}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$12{\text{n}} + 2{\text{n}} + 16{\text{n}} = 180$
$\therefore 30\,{\text{n}} = 180$
${\text{n}} = 6$
subject: Chemistry

question: 1 molal urea aqueous solution freezes at $-1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $9.00 \%(w / w)$ aqueous solution of a non-electrolyte A freezes at $-0.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Osmotic pressure of $4.41 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{v})$ aqueous solution at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is -atmosphere.
explanation:
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}$ molality
$1.86=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times 1$
$\therefore {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}} = 1.86\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{kg}}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{1000\,\,{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{1}}}}}$
$0.93^\circ = \frac{{1000 \times 1.86 \times 9}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}} \times 100}}$
${{\text{M}}_{\text{2}}}$ (molar mass of A) $ = 180\,{\text{g}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\pi($ osmotic pressure $)=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{RT}=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{\mathrm{mV}} \mathrm{RT}$
$=\dfrac{4.41 \times 0.0821 \times 298}{180 \times 0.1}=6\mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The freezing point depression of a $1.00 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,$ molal aqueous aqueous solution of ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{x}}}\left[ {{\rm{Fe}}{{({\rm{CN}})}_6}} \right]$ is $7.10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\rm{K}}.$ If ${{\rm{K}}_{{\rm{f }}}}\left( {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}} \right)$  $ = 1.86\,{\rm{K}}\,{\rm{K}}\,\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}},$ what is the value of x ?
explanation:
${{\text{K}}_{\text{x}}}\left[ {{\text{Fe}}{{({\text{CN}})}_6}} \right] \rightleftharpoons $
$\underbrace {{\text{x}}{{\text{K}}^ + } + {{\left[ {{\text{Fe}}{{({\text{CN}})}_6}} \right]}^{{\text{x}} - }}}_{{\text{n}} = {\text{ number of ions }}} = ({\text{x}} + 1)$
$\therefore {\kern 1pt} \,\,\,{\text{i}} = $ van't Hoff factor $ = 1 + ({\text{n}} - 1)\,\alpha $
Where, α = degree of ionization
$ = 1 = 1 + ({\text{x}} + 1 - 1)1 = ({\text{x}} + 1)$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {\text{molality}} \times {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times {\text{i}}$
$7 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 1.86 \times ({\text{x}} + 1)$
${\text{x}} + 1 = 3.76 \times 4$
$\therefore {\text{x}} = 3$
subject: Chemistry

question: When 0.2 mole of urea is dissolved in 100 g of a solvent, the depression of freezing point is ${\mathrm{0.50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$. When 0.3 mole of non-volatile solute is dissolved in 100 g of the same solvent, the depression of freezing point will be ____ $^\circ {\text{C}}$.
explanation:
For the same solvent, the value of ${\mathrm{K_{f}}}$ remains the same.
${\mathrm{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{1}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}_{1}}}$
${\mathrm{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{2}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}_{2}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \dfrac{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}=\dfrac{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{2}=\dfrac{\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}} \times \mathrm{m}_{2}}}$
$\therefore \quad {\left( {\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{0}}.{\text{50}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\text{0}}.{\text{2}}}}{{{\text{0}}.{\text{100}}}}} \right)}} \times \left( {\frac{{{\text{0}}.{\text{3}}}}{{{\text{0}}.{\text{100}}}}} \right){\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{0}}.{\text{50}} \times {\text{3}}}}{{\text{2}}}$
${\mathrm{=0.75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The freezing point of 2 molal aqueous solution of non-electrolyte is _____ $^\circ {\text{C}}$.
${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}\right.}}$ of water ${\mathrm{\left.=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)}}$
explanation:
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}=1.86 \times 2=3.72 \mathrm{~K}}}$ or ${\mathrm{3.72{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{0}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-3.72=-3.72{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
In a 0.2 molal aqueous solution of a weak acid
$\mathrm{HX}$ the degree of ionization is 0.3 . Taking $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for
water as 1.85 , the freezing point of the solution
will be nearest to
explanation:
$\left( {\mathop {{\text{HX}}}\limits_{{\text{1 - 0}}{\text{.3}}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {\text{H}}\limits_{{\text{0}}{\text{.3}}} }\limits^{\text{ + }} {\text{ + }}{{\mathop {\text{X}}\limits_{{\text{0}}{\text{.3}}} }^{\text{ - }}},{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{i = 1}}{\text{.3}}} \right)$
${\rm{i = 1 - 0}}{\rm{.3 + 0}}{\rm{.3 + 0}}{\rm{.3 = 1}}{\rm{.3}}$
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ \times m \times i}}\,\,{\rm{;}}$
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.85 \times 0}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{\rm{.3 = 0}}{\rm{.480^\circ C}}$
$\therefore {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = 0 - 0}}{\rm{.480 = - 0}}{\rm{.480^\circ C}}$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Coolant used in car radiator is aqueous solution
of ethylene glycol. In order to prevent the
solution from freezing at $-0.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How much
ethylene glycol must be added to $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water?
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \,\mathrm{Kg} \,\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.3^\circ C}}$
${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{A}}}}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.3 = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.86 \times }}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{62 \times 5000}}}}$
$\therefore {{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ = 50\,g}}$
The amount used should be more than 50 g.
subject: Chemistry

question: Camphor is often used in molecular mass determination because
explanation:
Camphor is used in molecular mass determination due to its volatile nature.The method is called Rast's camphor method. Camphor acts as a solid solvent which is volatile, hence can be removed easily.

question: The molal freezing point constant for water is $1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$. Therefore, the freezing point of  0.1 M NaCl solution in water is expected to be
explanation:

question: Equimolal solution of A and B shows depression in freezing point in the ratio of $2: 1$. ' A ' remains in normal state in solution. 'B' will be ____  in state in solution.
explanation:
$\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = {\rm{i}}{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{f}}} \cdot {\rm{m}}$
$\frac{{{{\left( {\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}} \right)}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\left( {\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}}} \right)}_{\rm{B}}}}} = \frac{{{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{A}}} \times {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}} \times {\rm{m}}}}{{{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{B}}} \times {{\rm{K}}_{\rm{f}}} \times {\rm{m}}}}$
$\dfrac{2}{1}=\dfrac{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}}}$
$\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{A}}=2 \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}}$
So, if A remains in normal state, and ${{\rm{i}}_{\rm{B}}}\,\, < \,\,\,{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{A}}}$ means B will associate in solution.
subject: Chemistry

question:
In the depression of freezing point experiment
A. vapour pressure of the solution is less than that of pure solvent
B. vapour pressure of the solution is more than that of pure solvent
C. only solute molecules solidify at the freezing point
D. only solvent molecules solidify at the freezing point.
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
explanation: Only statements A and D are correct. Vapour pressure of the solution is always less than that of the pure solvent. Also, only solvent molecules solidify.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: The water pouch of instant cold pack for treating athletic injury breaks when squeezed and $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ dissolved lowers the temperature.


Reason: Addition of non-volatile solute into solvent results into the depression of freezing point of the solvent.
explanation: Squeezing an instant cold pack releases ammonium nitrate, increasing solution density and lowering it's temperature. Ammonium nitrate addition reduces solvent vapor pressure, lowering freezing point, as freezing occurs when liquid and solid vapor pressures are equal. So, Option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Increasing pressure on pure water decreases its freezing point.

Reason: Density of water is maximum at $273{\text{ K}}.$
explanation: Water reaches maximum density at $4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ due to the arrangement of molecules. When pressure increases, water molecules cannot be properly arranged, so water cannot freeze at the freezing point at $273 \mathrm{~K}$. That means water freezes at below $273 \mathrm{~K}$. Hence, the given statement "Increasing pressure on pure water decreases its freezing point" is true. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: Molecular mass of polymers cannot be calculated by using boiling point or freezing point method.


Reason: Polymer solutions possess a low boiling point or freezing point.
explanation: Polymer solutions show minimal changes in boiling and freezing points due to the large size of polymer molecules. Measurement of osmotic pressure method is preferred for the determination of molar masses of polymers as it is carried out at room temperature, which does not require heating. Heating may change the nature of the polymer. So option (3)is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Sodium chloride used to clear snow on the roads.

Reason: Sodium chloride depresses the freezing point of water.
explanation: The addition of a non-volatile solute to a volatile solvent lowers the freezing point of the solvent. Thus, addition of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ decreases water's freezing point and prevents ice formation. So option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Larger the value of cryoscopic constant of the solvent, lesser will be the freezing point of the solution.


Reason: Extent of depression in the freezing point depends on the nature of the solvent.
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}$
$\Delta T_{f} \propto K_{f}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\text {solvent }}-\mathrm{T}_{\text {solution }}$
As $\mathrm{T}_{\text {solvent }}$ is constant, larger the value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$, lower the $\mathrm{T}_{\text {solution }}$. Also $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ depends on the nature of the solvent. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
An aqueous solution of a compound $A B$ freezes at $0.48^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At this temperature, $\mathrm{AB}$ behaves as a non-electrolyte. The same solution boils at $100.26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at a P of $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ (for water, $\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}=3.7$ ).
We can conclude that compound $\mathrm{AB}$ at the boiling point of solution,
explanation:
Apply, $\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{\text{m}}$
$0.48 = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times {\text{m}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(1)}}$
and $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{iK}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{m}$
${\text{0}}{\text{.26 = i}}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{\text{m}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
from eq 1,2 ,
$\dfrac{0.48}{0.26}=\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{i}} \times 3.7 \quad\left(\because \dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}=3.7\right)$
$\mathrm{i}=\dfrac{3.7}{1.84}=2$
Since vant hoff factor, $\mathrm{i}>1$, its a case of dissociation. It behaves like an electrolyte.
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is the normal b.p. of an aqueous solution whose freezing point is $-2.48^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{mol}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.512^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{mol}\right)$
explanation:
$\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2.48}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\dfrac{1.86}{0.512}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{2.48 \times 0.512}{1.86}=0.68$
$\approx 0.7$
$\therefore \mathrm{Tb}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{0}+\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=100+0.7$
$=100.7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The order of boiling points of four equimolar aqueous solutions is $\mathrm{C} < \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{A} < \mathrm{D}$. The correct order of their freezing points is:
explanation:
Elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point are the colligative properties. As the number of particles increases, boiling point increases but freezing point decreases since, the order of boiling point is $\mathrm{C} < \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{A} < \mathrm{D}$
$\therefore$ The order of freezing point is
$\mathrm{D} < \mathrm{A} < \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Calculate the molar mass of solute when 1.5 gram non volatile solute dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ solvent having density $0.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ lowers its freezing point by $0.75 \mathrm{~K}$.
(Freezing point depression constant for solvent is $5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Given,
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0.75 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{w}_{2}=$ weight of solute $=1.5 \mathrm{~g}$,
$\mathrm{w}_{1}=$ weight of solvent
$=\mathrm{V} \times \mathrm{d}=100 \times 0.8=80 \mathrm{~g}$
Depression of freezing point,
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{w}_{1}}$
[where $\mathrm{M}_{2}=$ Molar mass of solute]
or, $\mathrm{M}_{2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{w}_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{w}_{1}}$
$=\dfrac{5 \times 1.5 \times 1000}{0.75 \times 80}=125 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is the molar mass of solute when 5 gram solute dissolved in 70 gram solvent lowers its freezing piont by $2.5 \mathrm{~K}$ ?
(cryoscopic constant for solvent is $4.9 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Depression in freezing point,
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{B}} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{A}}} \\& \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{4.9}{2.5} \times \dfrac{5 \times 1000}{70}=\dfrac{24500}{175}=140 \\& \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}=140 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the mass of solute having molar mass $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, when dissolved in 98 gram solvent decreases its freezing point by $0.2 \mathrm{~K}$ ?(The numerical value of cryoscopic constant of solvent is 1.17)
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{2} \times 1000}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{w}_{1}}$
Given, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} 1.17, \mathrm{w}_{1}=98 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}=60, \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0.2 \mathrm{~K}$
$\therefore 0.2=1.17 \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{2} \times 1000}{60 \times 98}$
or, $\quad \mathrm{w}_{2}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 60 \times 98}{1.17 \times 1000} \mathrm{~g}=1.005 \mathrm{~g} \approx 1.0 \mathrm{~g}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The solution containing $6 \mathrm{~g}$ urea (molar mass $60)$ per $\mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of water and another solution containing $9 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute  A  per $\mathrm{dm}^{3}$ water freezes at same temperature. What is molar mass of A?
explanation:
Both solutions have same freezing point.
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$, so
$\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{2 \text { (urea) }}}{\mathrm{M}_{2(\mathrm{~A})}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2(\text { urea })}}{\mathrm{W}_{2(\mathrm{~A})}}[\because$ solvent is same $]$
$\therefore \dfrac{60}{\mathrm{M}_{2(\mathrm{~A})}}=\dfrac{6}{9}$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}_{2(\mathrm{~A})}=\dfrac{60 \times 9}{6}=90$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the freezing point of 1 molal aqueous solution of a non volatile solute?$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)\left(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{0}\right.$ for water $\left.=0^{0} \mathrm{C}\right)$
explanation:
${\text{m}} = 1{\text{mol k}}{{\text{g}}^{ - 1}}, {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = 1.86 {\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
${\text{T}}_{\text{f}}^0 = {0^0}{\text{C }}\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}{\text{m}}$
$ = 1.86 {\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} \times 1{\text{mol k}}{{\text{g}}^{ - 1}}$
$=1.86 \mathrm{~K}=1.86{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$ = 1.86\,{\text{K}} = 1.86^\circ {\text{C}}$
Now, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{0}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{0}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$=0{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-1.86{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=-1.86{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following formulae is used to obtain depression in freezing point?
explanation: Depression in freezing point, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $5 \mathrm{~g}$ sucrose (molar mass $=342)$ is dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of solvent, decreases the freezing point by $2.15 \mathrm{~K}$. What is cryoscopic constant of solvent?
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=5 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}=342 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$,$\mathrm{W}_{1}=100 \mathrm{~g}, \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=2.15 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{f}}=$ ?
Depression in freezing point,
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \dfrac{1000 \mathrm{~W}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~W}_{1}} \\& \therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~W}_{1}}{1000 \mathrm{~W}_{2}} \\& \therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=\dfrac{2.15 \mathrm{~K} \times 342 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 100 \mathrm{~g}}{1000 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \times 5 \mathrm{~g}} \\& \therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=14.7 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If $6 \mathrm{~g}$ solute dissolved in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water lowers the freezing point by $0.93 \mathrm{~K}$. what is molar mass of solute? $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=6 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{~W}_{1}=100 \mathrm{~g}$,
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0.93 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{M}_{2}=? \\& \quad \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m} \text { (or) } \\& \mathrm{M}_{2}=\dfrac{1000 \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{W}_{2}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{W}_{1}} \\& =\dfrac{1000 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \times 1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 6 \mathrm{~g}}{0.93 \mathrm{~K} \times 100 \mathrm{~g}} \\& \therefore \mathrm{M}_{2}=120 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Molal depression constant for a liquid is $2.77^{\circ} \mathrm{Ckg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$, in Kelvin scale it's value is
explanation: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ value will be same in kelvin or ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ i.e., $2.77 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

question: What is cryoscopic constant of water if $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water has depression in freezing point $2.15 \mathrm{~K}$? (Molar mass of glucose $=180$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=5 \mathrm{~g}, \quad \mathrm{~W}_{1}=100 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{M}_{2}=180 \mathrm{~g}, \quad \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=2.15 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=$ ?
Depression in freezing point, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \cdot \mathrm{m}($ or $)$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\frac{{1000 {{\text{W}}_2}}}{{{{\text{M}}_2} {{\text{W}}_1}}}$
$\therefore {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{M}}_2} {{\text{W}}_1}}}{{1000 {{\text{W}}_2}}} = \frac{{2.15 \times 180 \times 100}}{{1000 \times 5}}$
$ = 7.74\,\,{\text{k}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: ‘${\text{x}}$’  ${\text{g}}$  of urea (molar mass $60\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}})$ is completely dissolved in ' y ' g of pure water and the solution boiled at 373.202 K . If the boiling point of pure water at 1.013 bar is 373.15 K , then $\mathrm{x}: \mathrm{y}$ is $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=373.202 \mathrm{~K}-373.15 \mathrm{~K}=0.052 \mathrm{~K}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times$ Molarity
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \dfrac{\text { Number of moles of solute (urea) }}{\text { Mass of solvent (in kg) }}$
$0.052=0.52 \times \dfrac{\dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{60}}{\dfrac{\mathrm{y}}{1000}}$
$0.052=0.52 \times \dfrac{\mathrm{x}}{60 \times \mathrm{y}} \times 1000$
$\frac{{0.052 \times 60}}{{0.52 \times 1000}} = \frac{{\text{x}}}{{\text{y}}}$
${\text{x}}:{\text{y}} = 6 \times {10^{ - 3}}.$
subject: Chemistry

question: 180 g of glucose, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$, is dissolved in 1 kg of water in a vessel. The temperature at which water boils at 1.013 bar is _____ (Given, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$; Boiling point for pure water is 373.15 K )
explanation:
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{ }^{\circ}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-373.15=1 \times 0.52 \times \dfrac{180}{180} \times \dfrac{1000}{1000}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=373.67 \mathrm{~K}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The boiling point of pure benzene is $80.1\,^\circ {\text{C}}$. When 2 g of a non-volatile solute (molar mass ${\mathrm{=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$ ) was dissolved in 100 g of benzene. The solution will boil at ____ $^\circ {\text{C}}$.
${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\right.}}$ for benzene ${\mathrm{\left.=2.53 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)}}$
explanation:
$\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{B}}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{A}}}}}$
${\rm{ = 2}}.{\rm{53}} \times \frac{{\rm{2}}}{{{\rm{60}} \times {\rm{0}}.{\rm{1}}}} = 0.84\,{\rm{K or }}0.84^\circ {\rm{C}}$
${\mathrm{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\circ}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=80.1+0.84=80.94^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Aqueous solution containing 2.20 mol urea in 0.50 kg of water will have the same boiling point that of aqueous solution containing ____ mol sucrose in 1 kg of water.
explanation: Both urea and sucrose are non-electrolyte. In order to have same boiling point, both aqueous solutions of urea and sucrose must be equimolal Aqueous solution containing 4.40 mol sucrose in 1 kg of water is equimolal to the aqueous solution containing 2.20 mol urea in 0.50 kg of water.

question: Boiling point of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}}$ was raised by 0.29 K when 0.5 g anthracene was dissolved in 35 g ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}}$. Molecular mass of anthracene is 178 u . The boiling point elevation constant ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}}$ is ____ ${\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$.
explanation: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\text { molality }}}}$;${\mathrm{\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}}}$${\mathrm{\therefore \quad=\dfrac{0.29 \times 178 \times 35 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5}}}$${\mathrm{=3.61 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The solution containing $3 \mathrm{~g}$ urea (molar mass 60) per $\mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of water and another solution containing $4.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute A per $\mathrm{dm}^{3}$ boils at same temperature, then what is molar mass of A?
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=3 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{W}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})=4.5 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})=$ ?
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{M}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\text { Mass of solvent }}$
So, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ is same.
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})}{\mathrm{M}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \mathrm{M}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})=\dfrac{\mathrm{M}_{2} \times \mathrm{W}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})}{\mathrm{W}_{2}} \\& =\dfrac{60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 4.5 \mathrm{~g}}{3 \mathrm{~g}}=90 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the boiling point of 0.5 molal aqueous solution of sucrose if 0.1 molal aqueous solution of glucose boils at $100.16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
explanation:
For glucose solution,
$\mathrm{m}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{b}}=100.16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{ }^{\mathrm{o}}$
$=100.16-100=0.16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\mathrm{m}}=\dfrac{0.16^{0} \mathrm{C}}{0.1 \mathrm{~m}}=1.6{ }^{0} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$
For sucrose solution, $\mathrm{m}=0.5 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.6^{0} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$ $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m}$
$=1.6{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \times 0.5 \mathrm{~m}=0.80{ }^{0} \mathrm{C}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\mathrm{o}}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}+\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\mathrm{o}}$
$=0.80+100=100.80{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is boiling point of a decimolal aqueous solution of glucose if molal elevation constant for water is $0.52^\circ {\text{C}}\,{\text{kg}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$?
explanation:
${\text{m}} = 0.1 {\text{m}}, {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = 0.52^\circ {\text{C kg mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \times {\text{m}} = 0.52^\circ {\text{C kg mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} \times 0.1{\text{mol k}}{{\text{g}}^{ - 1}} = 0.052^\circ {\text{C}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{0} \quad \therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}+\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{0} \\& \therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.052+100=100.052^{0} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A solution of $6 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water boils at $100.52^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The molal elevation constant of water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. What is molar mass of solute?
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=6 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{~W}_{1}=100 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=100.52^{0} \mathrm{C}$
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}} & =\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}^{\mathrm{o}}=(100.52+273)-(100+273) \\& =0.52 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$
Elevation in boiling point, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{m}$ (or)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{M}_{2}=\dfrac{1000 \cdot \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{W}_{2}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{W}_{1}} \\& =\dfrac{1000 \mathrm{gkg}^{-1} \times 0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 6 \mathrm{~g}}{0.52 \mathrm{~K} \times 100 \mathrm{~g}}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}_{2}=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If boiling point of water is $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How many gram of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ is added in $500 \mathrm{~g}$ of water to increase its boiling point by approx $1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\right.$ of water $=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \times \mathrm{kg} /$ mole $)$.
explanation:
For the solutes, which can undergo association or dissociation in a given solvent,
Elevation in boiling point
$\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)=\mathrm{iK}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m}$,
where, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=$ molal elevation constant
$\mathrm{m}=$ molality, $\mathrm{i}=$ van't Hoff factor
Given,
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{NaCl})=58.5$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
for $\mathrm{NaCl} ; \mathrm{n}=2$ and $\mathrm{W}_{\text {solvent }}=500 \mathrm{~g}$
Thus, $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}(\text { solute })}{\mathrm{M}(\text { solute })} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}(\text { solvent })}$
$1=2 \times 0.52 \times \dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{58.5} \times \dfrac{1000}{500}$
$\mathrm{w}=\dfrac{58.5 \times 500}{2 \times 0.52 \times 1000}=28.125 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NaCl}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: If boiling point of urea solution is 100. ${\rm{1}}{{\rm{8}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$ and ${{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}$ for water is ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.512}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{K}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$, molality of solution is (Boiling point of water ${\rm{ = 10}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$)

question:

question: The correct relation between elevation of boiling point and molar mass of solute is
explanation:
For dilute solutions, the elevation in boiling point is directly proportional to the molal concentration of the solute in solution (m)
Thus,
$\Delta {T_b} \propto m$
$\Delta {T_b} = {K_b}m$
Where, ${K_b}$ is molal boiling point elevation constant. As molality $\left( {\rm{m}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}\left( {{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right)}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solvent}}\left( {{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)\left( {{\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kg}}} \right)}}$
${\rm{Moles}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}\left( {{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times }}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{1}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{k}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{.}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{.}}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{1}}}}}$

question: The amount of $50 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ solution of hydrochloric acid required to react with 200 g of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ would be
explanation:
$50 \% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}$ solution of HCl solution means that 50 g of HCl is present in 100 g solution. $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
1 mole of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ reacts with 2 moles of HCl
100 g of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=1$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
200 g of moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=2$ moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$
2 moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ will react with 4 moles of HCl
1 mole of $\mathrm{HCl}=36.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of HCl
4 moles of HCl $=4 \times 36.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}=146 \mathrm{~g}$ of HCl
50 g of HCl is present in 100 g solution.
146 g of HCl will be present in 292 g solution.
subject: Chemistry

question: An aqueous solution of  $9.8\,\% \,\,({\text{w}}/{\text{w}})$  ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$   has density of $1.02 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$. The molality of the solution is
explanation:
$9.8 \% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}$ solution density is $1.02 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$
100 cc solution mass is 102 g .
Mass of solute $ = \frac{{9.8}}{{100}} \times 102 = 9.996\,{\text{g}}\,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$
Mass of solvent $=92.004 \mathrm{~g}$
Molality $=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1000}{\text { Mass of solvent }}$
$=\dfrac{9.996}{98} \times \dfrac{1000}{92.004}=1.1 \mathrm{~m}$
Molality $=1.1 \mathrm{~m}$
subject: Chemistry
