question: How much volume of 1 N aqueous solution of   $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ should be taken, which will contain 0.2 moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ ?
explanation:

$1 \mathrm{~N}=0.5 \mathrm{M}$
${\text{M}} = {\text{n}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{{\text{V}}({\text{mL}})}}$
$0.5 = 0.2 \times \frac{{1000}}{{{\text{V}}({\text{mL}})}}$ $\mathrm{V}=\dfrac{2 \times 1000}{5}=400 \mathrm{~mL}$

question: A litre of sea water (which weights 1030 g) contains $6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g}$ of dissolved oxygen. The concentration of dissolved oxygen in ppm is
explanation:
Parts per million $ = \frac{{{\text{ Number of parts of component }}}}{\begin{gathered} {\text{ Total number of parts of}} \hfill \\ {\text{ all components }} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} } \times {10^6}$
$\therefore$ Concentration of dissolved oxygen in ppm
$=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{1030} \times 10^{6}=\dfrac{6}{1030} \times 10^{3}$
$=0.005825 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{ppm}=5.8 \mathrm{ppm}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 0.1 mole of NaCl is dissolved in 100 g of water. The mole  fraction of NaCl is
explanation:

Mole fraction of NaCl $=\dfrac{\text { No. of moles of } \mathrm{NaCl}}{\text { Total no. of moles }}$
$=\dfrac{0.1}{5.55+0.1}=\dfrac{0.1}{5.65}=0.0177$

question: Density of 3 M NaOH is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml}$. Molality of solution is
explanation:
${\rm{m}} = \frac{{1000\,{\rm{M}}}}{{1000\,{\rm{d}} - \left( {{\rm{M}} \times {{\rm{M}}_2}} \right)}}$
(where $\mathrm{M}=$ molarity, $\mathrm{m}=$ molality, $\mathrm{d}=$ density of solution, $\mathrm{M}_{2}=$ molar mass of the solute)
$\therefore \mathrm{m}=\dfrac{3 \times 1000}{1250-(3 \times 40)}=\dfrac{3 \times 1000}{1130}=2.65 \mathrm{~m}$

$\text { Normality }=\dfrac{\text { gram equivalent of solute }}{\text { volume of solution (in } \mathrm{L} \text { ) }}$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{NaOH}=23+16+1$
$=40$
Volume of solution
$ = 100\,{\rm{mL}} = 100/1000\,{\rm{L}}$
$ = 0.1\,{\rm{L}}$
$\therefore {\rm{ Normality }} = \frac{{4/40}}{{0.1}} = 1.0\,{\rm{N}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $\,138{\rm{g}}$ ethyl alcohol is mixed with $72\,{\rm{g}}$  of water. The ratio of mole fraction of alcohol to water is
explanation:
Number of moles of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$
$ = \frac{{138}}{{46}} = 3\left( {{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{C}}_2}{{\rm{H}}_5}{\rm{OH}}}}{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{46\,g/mol}}} \right)$
Number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\dfrac{72}{18}=4$
$\left( {\because {{\text{M}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{0}}}} = 18\,{\text{g}}/{\text{mol}}} \right)$
Mole fraction, $\chi_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}=\dfrac{3}{3+4}=\dfrac{3}{7}$
${\chi _{{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}}}} = \frac{4}{{3 + 4}} = \frac{4}{7}$
$\frac{{{\chi _{{{\rm{C}}_2}{{\rm{H}}_5}{\rm{OH}}}}}}{{{\chi _{{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}}}}}} = \frac{{3/7}}{{4/7}}$
$ = 3/4 = 3:4$
subject: Chemistry

question: How much volume of $3.0\,\,{\rm{M}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is required for the preparation of 1.0 litre of 1.0 M solution?
explanation:
Dilution law,
$\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}$
$3 \times V_{1}=1 \times 1$
$\mathrm{V}_{1}=\dfrac{1}{3}$ litre $\approx 333.3 \mathrm{~mL}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A solution of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ is $0.5 \mathrm{~mol} /$ liter ; then the moles of chloride ion in 500 mL will be
explanation:
1 litre solution $\rightarrow 0.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$
0.5 litre solution
$\frac{{0.5}}{2}\,{\rm{mol}}\,\,{\rm{CaC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$.
Moles of $\mathrm{C} \overline{1}$ ion $=\dfrac{0.5}{2} \times 2=0.5$ mole ${\rm{C\bar l}}$ ions.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the mass ratio of ethylene glycol ${\text{(}}{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}{{\text{O}}_2},$ molar mass $\left.=62 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\right)$ required for making 500 g of 0.25 molal aqueous solution and 250 mL of 0.25 molal aqueous solution?
explanation:
Assume: Mass of solvent $\approx$ Mass of solution
0.25 molal $=\dfrac{\text { No. of moles }}{500 \mathrm{~g}} \times 1000$
No. of moles $=\dfrac{0.25}{2}$
$\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Molar mass }}=\dfrac{0.25}{2}$
$\Rightarrow(\text { Mass })_{1}=\dfrac{0.25}{2} \times 62=7.75 \mathrm{~g}$
$(\because$ density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}, 1 \mathrm{~g}=1 \mathrm{cc})$
0.25 molal $=\dfrac{\text { No. of } \text { moles }}{250(\mathrm{~mL})} \times 1000$
No. of moles $=\dfrac{0.25}{4}$
$\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Molar mass }}=\dfrac{0.25}{4}$
$\Rightarrow(\text { Mass })_{2}=\dfrac{0.25}{4} \times 62=3.875 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass $_{1}:$ Mass $_{2}=7.75: 3.875=2: 1$
subject: Chemistry

question: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of " ${\text{X}}$ " to 1 mole of water. Mass percent of " ${\text{X}}$ " in the solution is
explanation:
Given amount of solute $({\rm{X}}) = 2\,{\rm{g}}$
Amount of solvent $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=1$ mole $=18 \mathrm{~g}$
Total mass of solution $=2+18=20 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, mass percentage of ${\rm{X}} = \frac{2}{{20}} \times 100 = 10\,\% $
Thus, mass percent of " ${\text{X}}$ " in the solution is $10 \%$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read Assertion and Reason carefully and mark correct option.

Assertion: 3.1500 g of hydrated oxalic acid dissolved in water to make 250.0 mL solution will result in 0.1 M oxalic acid solution.

Reason: Molar mass of hydrated oxalic acid is $126 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
explanation:
${\rm{n}} = \frac{{{\rm{ Mass }}}}{{{\rm{ Molar}}\,\,{\rm{mass }}}} = \frac{{3.1500}}{{126}} = 0.025\,\,{\rm{moles}}$
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}} \times 1000=\dfrac{0.025}{250} \times 1000=0.1 \mathrm{M}$
Thus, both assertion and reason are true and reason is the corect explanation of assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.
Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: The molality of the solution does not change with change in temperature.

Reason: The molality is expressed in units of moles per $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of solvent.
explanation: Molality is moles of solute in $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass of solvent. It is temperature independent as it is not influenced by volume fluctuations. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: When a concentrated solution is diluted by adding more water, the number of moles of solute in the solution remains unchanged.


Reason: Number of moles of a solute is equal to the product of molarity and volume of solution in litres.
explanation: The moles of solute in a solution remain constant when additional solvent is added, as the increase in volume proportionally decreases the molarity to maintain a consistent product of moles and molarity. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Concentration Terms and Temperature Dependence

question:
Assertion: One molar aqueous solution has always higher concentration than one molal.


Reason: The molality of a solution depends upon the density of the solution whereas molarity does not.
explanation: Molarity is one mole solute dissolved in 1L of solution. Whereas, molality is one mole solute disssolved in $1 \mathrm{Kg}$ solvent. Since volume of solution includes volume of both solute and solvent, the solute is present in lesser quantity in molar solution than that present in molal solution. Molality depends upon the density of the solution whereas molarity does not. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Concentration Terms and Temperature Dependence

question:
Assertion: One molal aqueous solution of urea contains $60 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea in $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water.


Reason: Solution containing one mole of solute in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ solvent is called one molal solution.
explanation:
Molecular weight of urea $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}\right)$ is 60. $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mole}$.
Number of moles $=\dfrac{\text { Weight }}{\text { Molecular weight }}=\dfrac{60}{60}=1$
$\mathrm{m}=\dfrac{\text { No.of moles of solute }}{\text { Mass of solvent in } \mathrm{kg}}=\dfrac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{1 \mathrm{~kg}}=1 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~mol}$ (or) $60 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea is present in $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of solvent.
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: If 0.01 mole of solute is present in $500 \mathrm{ml}$ of solution, its molarity is
explanation: $\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n} \times 1000}{\text { vol. in } \mathrm{ml}}=\dfrac{0.01 \times 1000}{500}=0.02 \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry

question: 10 millimoles of solute is present in the following volume of $0.08 \mathrm{M}$ solution
explanation:
No of mill moles $=\mathrm{M} \times$ vol.in $\mathrm{mL}$
$\therefore \mathrm{vol}=\dfrac{10}{0.08}=125 \mathrm{~mL}$
subject: Chemistry

question: $2.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of $0.2\,{\text{M }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$ solution is diluted to $0.5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$. The normality of the diluted solution is
explanation:
$\mathrm{M}_{1}=0.2 \mathrm{M}$
$\therefore \mathrm{N}_{1}=2 \times \mathrm{M}_{1}=0.4 \mathrm{~N}$
Dilution Law,
${{\text{N}}_1}\,{{\text{V}}_1} = {{\text{N}}_2}\,{{\text{V}}_2}$
${{\text{V}}_1} = 2.5\,{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^3} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{d}}{{\text{m}}^3};$
$\mathrm{V}_{2}=0.5 \mathrm{dm}^{3}, \mathrm{~N}_{1}=0.4 ; \mathrm{N}_{2}=?$
$0.4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{N}_{2} \times 0.5$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}=2 \times 10^{-3}=0.002 \mathrm{~N}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Molarity of $0.1 \mathrm{~N}$ oxalic acid is
explanation:
Oxalic acid is a dibasic acid.
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{M} \times$ basicity
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\mathrm{N}}{\text { Basicity }}=\dfrac{0.1}{2}=0.05 \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Concentration Terms and Temperature Dependence

question: In a normal solution of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$, normalities of $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$are in the ratio of
explanation: Gram equivalents are always equal
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the mole fraction of glucose in $10 \% \mathrm{w} /$ w glucose solution?
explanation:
No. of moles of glucose $=\dfrac{10}{180}=0.0555$
No. of moles of water $=\dfrac{90}{18}=5$
$\therefore$ No. of moles of solution $=5.0555$
Mole fraction of glucose $=$
$\frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of moles of glucose}}}}{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of moles of solution}}}}$
$=\dfrac{0.0555}{5.0555}=0.01$
subject: Chemistry

question: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving $120 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (molecular mass $=60 \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $1.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml}$. The molarity of this solution is:
explanation:
Mass of solute taken $=120 \mathrm{~g}$
Molecular mass of solute $=60 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of solvent $=1000 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of solution $=1000+120=1120 \mathrm{~g}$
Volume of solution $=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { Density }}=\dfrac{1120}{1.15} \mathrm{ml}$
$=973.9 \mathrm{ml}=0.9739 \mathrm{~L}$
Now, no. of moles of solute $=\dfrac{120 \mathrm{~g}}{60 \mathrm{~g}}=2$
$\therefore$ Molarity $=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{L})}=\dfrac{2}{0.9739}=2.05 \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The molarity of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ solution, which has a density $1.84 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$ at $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and contains $98 \%$ by weight is
explanation:
$98 \%$ by mass means ${\text{98 }}\,{\text{g }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}$ is present in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of acid.
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\text { given mass } \times 1000}{\text { molecular mas } \times \mathrm{V}_{(\mathrm{cc})}}$
Given, mass $=98 \mathrm{~g}$,
molecular mass $=98 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{V}=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\dfrac{100}{1.84}$
( $\because$ density of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is 1.84 )
Putting the values,
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{98 \times 1000 \times 1.84}{98 \times 100}=18.4 \mathrm{M}$
(OR)
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w} \times \mathrm{d} \times 10}{\text { Molar mass }}=\dfrac{98 \times 1.84 \times 10}{98}=18.4 \mathrm{M}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Relation between Concentration Terms and Temperature Dependence

question: The normality of orthophosphoric acid having purity of $70 \%$ by weight and specific gravity 1.54 is
explanation:
$70 \%$ by weight means $70 \mathrm{~g}$ of orthophosphoric acid is present in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of acid.
$\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{\mathrm{Eq} \cdot \mathrm{wt}} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}_{(\mathrm{cc})}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{w}=70 \mathrm{~g}$
Orthophosphoric acid is $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$. It is tribasic acid.
$\therefore$ E.q. wt.
$=\dfrac{\text { molecular mass }}{\text { number of replaceable } \mathrm{H} \text { - atoms }}=\dfrac{98}{3}$
$\mathrm{V}=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\dfrac{100}{1.54}$
Putting values in Eq. (1)
$\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{70 \times 3 \times 1000 \times 1.54}{98 \times 100}=33 \mathrm{~N}$
(OR)
$\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{\% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w} \times \mathrm{d} \times 10}{\mathrm{GEW}}=\dfrac{70 \times 1.54 \times 10}{98 / 3}=33 \mathrm{~N}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: $1.575 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ in $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ solution makes it $0.1 \mathrm{~N}$.

Reason: $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is a dihydrate organic acid.
explanation:
Normality $=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { eq.mass }} \times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~mL})}$
(eq.mass of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is 63 as it is a dibasic acid)
$\mathrm{N}=\dfrac{1.575}{63} \times \dfrac{1000}{250}=0.1 \mathrm{~N}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is a dihydrate organic acid, since it contains 2 molecules of water of crystallisation. But it is not the required explanation.

options.text: If both Assertion & Reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
If both Assertion & Reason are true but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false

(molecular weight of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 106}}\,{\rm{g/mol}}$)
explanation:
${\rm{Initial}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{conc}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.65}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{per}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{250}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{cc = }}$
${\rm{10}}{\rm{.60g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ = }}\left( {{\rm{10}}{\rm{.6/106}}} \right){\rm{mol}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{L}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.1M}}$.
When 10 mL is diluted to one litre, dilution = 100 times.
Hence, final conc. ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.1}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.001}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{M}}$.

question:
Three statements are given about mole fraction
i. Mole fraction of a solute + mole fraction of solvent = 1
ii. Equal weights of Helium and methane are present in a gaseous mixture. The mole fraction of He is 4/5
iii. The mole fraction of $40\% $ by mass of NaOH solution is 0.5
explanation:
(i) Sum of the mole fraction of all the components in solution is 1.
(ii) Let us assume weight of ${\rm{HE}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}$ as 1 g Mole fraction of ${\rm{HE = }}\frac{{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}}}{{\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{6}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{5}}}$.
(iii) 40% by mass of NaOH means 40 g of NaOH present in 100 g of solution.
Mole fraction of ${\rm{NaOH = }}\frac{{\frac{{{\rm{40}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{{\rm{40}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}} \right){\rm{ + }}\left( {\frac{{{\rm{60}}}}{{{\rm{18}}}}} \right)}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.23}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A 5.2 molal aqueous solution of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{OH}}$ is supplied. What is the mole fraction of methyl alcohol in the solution?
explanation:
We know, ${\rm{m = }}\frac{{{{\rm{x}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{\left( {{\rm{1 - }}{{\rm{x}}_{\rm{B}}}} \right){{\rm{m}}_{\rm{A}}}}}$
Given: ${\rm{m = 5}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{m}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = 18}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{5}}{\rm{.2 = }}\frac{{{{\rm{x}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{\left( {{\rm{1 - }}{{\rm{x}}_{\rm{B}}}} \right){\rm{ \times 18}}}}$
${\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{x}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.086}}$

question: An X molal solution of a compound in benzene has mole fraction of solute = 0.2. The value of X is
explanation:
Mole fraction of solute ${{\rm{X}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2}}$.
Therefore, mole fraction of solvent ${{\rm{X}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}$
${\rm{Or}}{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{0}}{\rm{.8}}$
${\rm{Or}}\,\,\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.8}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{4}}}$
Now, if ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}$ (solvent moles) = 1000/78 = 12.8 moles
${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{12}}{\rm{.8}}}}{{\rm{4}}}$ =3.2moles. Therefore, 3.2 moles of the compound are present in one Kg of solvent benzene and so molality = 3.2.

${\rm{Molarity}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{urea = }}\frac{{{\rm{15g}}}}{{{\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.5L}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{0}}{\rm{.5}}\,\,{\rm{mol/L = 0}}{\rm{.5}}\,\,{\rm{mol/d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
$\left( {{\rm{1L = 1d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}} \right)$

question: ${\rm{60g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{COOH}}$  dissolved in ${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ solvent, what is molality of solution? $\left( {{\rm{density = 1}}{\rm{.25g/c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}} \right)$
explanation:
Given,
${\rm{Volume = 1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{d}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{density = 1}}{\rm{.25g/c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
${\rm{Mass = 60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
We know that, molality $\left( {\rm{M}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}}}{{{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solvents}}}}$
$\therefore {\rm{Moles = }}\frac{{{\rm{Given}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{60g}}}}{{{\rm{60g/mol}}}}$
${\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}$
${\rm{molality}}\left( {\rm{m}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{Moles}}}}{{{\rm{Volume \times Density}}}}$
$m = \frac{1}{{1000c{m^3} \times 1.25g/c{m^3}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.25g \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{3}}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.25kg}}}}$
${\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{m}}$

question: At $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ urea solution has an osmotic pressure of 400 mm . On dilution by x times, its osmotic pressure decreased to 100 mm at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The dilution factor x is approximately
explanation:
$\pi_{1}=\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{RT}_{1}$
$400 = {{\text{C}}_1}{\text{R}} \times 273\,{\text{K}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\pi_{2}=\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{RT}_{2}$
$100 = {{\text{C}}_2}{\text{R}} \times 293\,{\text{K}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
Dividing equation (1) and (2),
$\dfrac{\pi_{1}}{\pi_{2}}=\dfrac{400}{100}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1} \times 273}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \times 293}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\dfrac{400 \times 293}{100 \times 273}=4.29 \approx 4.3$
$\mathrm{C}_{1}$ is 4.3 times $\mathrm{C}_{2}$.
So, the dilution factor is 4.3.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the effect of external pressure on the osmotic pressure (OP) of a solution?
explanation:
Osmotic pressure is a colligative property. So, it depends only on the concentration or amount of solute and temperature.
So, osmotic pressure of a solution remains unchanged on increasing or decreasing the external pressure.

options.text: OP decreases with increase of pressure
OP remained nearly same with increase/ decrease of external pressure
OP increases with increase of pressure

question: A 3 mL of solution was made by dissolving 20 mg of protein at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The osmotic pressure of the resulting solution is 3.8 torr. The molecular weight of the protein is approximately (in $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$ )
explanation:
$\pi=\mathrm{CRT}$
$\dfrac{3.8}{760}=\dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{\text { Molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1000}{3} \times 0.0821 \times 273$
$\therefore$ Molar mass $=29884 \approx 3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~g} \cdot \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

question: $15 \%$ aqueous solution of glucose (molecular weight $=180 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$) is isotonic with $8 \%$ aqueous solution containing an unknown non - dissociable solute. What is the molecular weight of the unknown solute?
explanation:
For isotonic solutions, $\mathrm{C}_{1}=\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$\dfrac{15}{180}=\dfrac{8}{\text { molar mass }}$
Molar mass $=\dfrac{180 \times 8}{15}=96 \mathrm{~g} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

question:
At 300 K , a one litre solution of sucrose (molecular weight: 342) was prepared by dissolving 40 g of sucrose. What is the approximate osmotic pressure (in kPa ) of solution at the same temperature?
( $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{PaK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\pi=\dfrac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Molar mass }} \times \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{~V}(\text { lit })} \times \mathrm{RT}$
$\pi=\dfrac{40}{342} \times \dfrac{1}{1} \times 8.314 \times 10^{6} \times 300$
$=292 \times 10^{6}=292 \mathrm{k} \cdot \mathrm{Pa}$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many grams of glucose must be added to 0.5 liter of a solution so that its osmotic pressure is same as that of a solution of 9.2 g of glucose dissolved in a liter?
explanation:
Same osmotic pressure means same concentration.
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{180} \times \dfrac{1}{0.5}=\dfrac{9.2}{180} \times \dfrac{1}{1}$
$\mathrm{W}=9.2 \times 0.5=4.6 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose.

question:
${\mathrm{0.05 \mathrm{M} \,\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}}}$ when treated with ${\mathrm{0.01 \mathrm{M}\, \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}}$ gives green colour solution of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}}$. Due to osmosis
Side X SPM Side ${\mathrm{Y}}$[SPM : Semi Permeable Membrane]Due to osmosis undefined
undefined undefined

question: The osmotic pressure of a solution containing 40 g of solute (molecular mass 246) per litre at ${\mathrm{27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}$ is ( ${\mathrm{R=0.0822 \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~L}}}$ ${\mathrm{\mathrm{mol}^{-1}}}$ )
explanation:
${\mathrm{\pi=C R T=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{W_{2}}{M w_{2}}\right) R T}{V}}}$
Given ${\mathrm{W_{2}=40 \mathrm{~g}}}$
${\mathrm{M w_{2}=246}}$
${\mathrm{T=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=300 \mathrm{~K}}}$
${\mathrm{V=1 \mathrm{~L}}}$
Substituting all the values, we get
${\mathrm{\pi=\dfrac{40}{246} \times 0.082 \times 300=4 \mathrm{~atm}}}$

question:
The Osmotic pressure of the aq. solution of the given solute at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ if depression in freezing point is $0.90^{\circ}$ is ____.(approx.)

explanation:
$\Delta {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{f}}} = {{\rm{k}}_{\rm{f}}} \times {\rm{molarity}},$ $\pi = {\rm{molarity}} \times {\rm{RT}}$

$\pi=11.918$ atm

$ = 0.3 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,{\rm{mol/L}}$

question: If osmotic pressure of $4\% ({\rm{w/V}})$ solution of sucrose is same as $2\% ({\rm{w/V}})$ solution of  ${\rm{'X'}}$, then the molecular mass of ${\rm{X}}$ (g/mol) is
explanation:
$4 \%$ solution of sucrose$({\rm{w/v}})$ means $4 \mathrm{~g}$ of sucrose is dissolved in 40 $\mathrm{mL}$ of solution. Similarly, $2\% ({\rm{w/v}})$ solution of ${\rm{'X'}}$ means $2 \mathrm{~g}$ of ${\rm{X}}$ present in $40 \mathrm{~mL}$ of solution.
As we know that, osmotic pressure${\rm{(\pi )}} = {\rm{CRT}}\,{\rm{or \pi }} = \frac{{\rm{n}}}{{\rm{V}}}{\rm{RT}}$.
where, ${\rm{n}} = $ number of moles of solute.
Since, ${\pi _1}$ (for sucrose solution) $ = {\pi _2}$
(for ${\rm{X}}$ solution)
$\therefore $$\therefore \frac{{{{\text{n}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{1}}}}}{\text{RT}} = \frac{{{{\text{n}}_{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{V}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{RT}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{4}{{342}} = \frac{2}{{{\text{Molecular}}\,\,{\text{weight}}}}$
$\left. {\left[ {\therefore {\rm{n}} = \frac{{{\rm{Mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{solute}}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}\,{\rm{mass}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{solute}}}}} \right.} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{ Molecular \,\,weight }} = \frac{2}{4} \times 342{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{Molecular \,\,weight }} = 171\,{\rm{g}}\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$

question: An electrolyte A  gives 3 ions and B  is a nonelectrolyte. If 0.1 M solution of B produces an osmotic pressure P , then 0.05 M solution of A will produce pressure, assuming that the electrolyte is completely ionised.

$\dfrac{\pi_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{3 \times 0.05}{1 \times 0.1} \Rightarrow \pi_{\mathrm{A}}=1.5 \mathrm{P}$

question: A 0.004 M solution of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is isotonic with a 0.010 M solution of glucose at same temperature. The apparent degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is
explanation:
$\pi=$ iCRT
$\pi_{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}=\pi_{\text {glucose }}$
$\mathrm{i} \times 0.004 \times \mathrm{RT}=1 \times 0.01 \times \mathrm{RT}$
$\mathrm{i}=\dfrac{0.01}{0.004}=2.5$
Degree of dissociation,
$\alpha=\dfrac{\mathrm{i}-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}$
$\mathrm{n}=3$ (3 ions)
$\alpha=\dfrac{2.5-1}{3-1}=\dfrac{1.5}{2}=0.75$ or $75 \%$

question: Osmotic pressure of urea solution at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 500 mm . Osmotic pressure of the solution becomes 105.3 mm , when it is diluted and temperature raised to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The extent dilution is

$\dfrac{\pi_{1}}{\pi_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{RT}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{RT}_{2}}$
$\dfrac{500}{105.3}=\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1} \times 283}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \times 298}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\dfrac{500}{105.3} \times \dfrac{298}{283}=5$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{5} \Rightarrow$ So, it is diluted 5 times.

question:
Evaluate the following statements for their correctness.
A. The elevation in boiling point temperature of water will be same for 0.1 M NaCl and 0.1 M urea.
B. Azeotropic mixtures boil without change in their composition.
C. Osmosis always takes place from hypertonic to hypotonic solution.
D. The density of $32\,\% \,\,{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4}$ solution havingmolarity 4.09 M is approximately $1.26 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$.
E. A negatively charged sol is obtained when KI solution is added to silver nitrate solution.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
(A) $\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} \propto {\text{i}} \times {\text{C}}$
(B) Azeotropic mixtures have same composition in both liquid and vapour phase.
(C) Osmosis always takes place from hypotonic to hypertonic solution.
(D) The density of $32\,\% \,\,{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4}$ solution having molarity 4.09 M is approximately $1.26 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$.
(E) When KI solution is added to $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution, positively charged sol is formed due to adsorption of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions from dispersion medium. Thus, only statements B and D are correct. Hence, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Reverse osmosis is used in the desalination of sea water.


Reason: When pressure more than osmotic pressure is applied, pure water is squeezed out of the sea water through the membrane.
explanation: Reverse osmosis occurs when higher pressure than osmotic pressure is applied, causing movement of pure solvent molecules from the solution side to the solvent side through a semi-permeable membrane. It is a process commonly used in water purification to remove contaminants by selectively allowing solvent molecules to pass through. So option (1) is correct.

question:

explanation: Colligative properties of a solution, like osmotic pressure and freezing point depression, depend solely on the number of solute particles, regardless of the solute's identity, as long as the particles remain undissociated and undergo no chemical changes in the solution. question:
A solution is made by dissolving $20 \mathrm{~g}$ of a substance in $500 \mathrm{ml}$ of water. Its osmotic pressure was found to be $600 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ at $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The molecular weight of the
substance is
explanation:
$\pi=\mathrm{CRT}$ (or)
$\mathrm{M}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{RT}}{\pi \mathrm{V}}$
$=\dfrac{20 \times 0.0821 \times 288 \times 1000}{(600 / 760) \times 500}=1198 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

question: Osmotic pressure of a solution containing $2 \mathrm{~g}$ dissolved protein per $300 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of solution is 20 $\mathrm{mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The molecular mass of the protein is:
explanation:
$\pi = \frac{{{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{RT}}}}{{\text{V}}}:{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{{\text{w}}_{\text{B}}}}}{{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}}$
$\pi=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{V}} \times \dfrac{\mathrm{RT}}{\pi}=\dfrac{2 \times 0.0821 \times 300 \times 760}{0.3 \times 20}$
$=6239.6 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Calculate osmotic pressure exerted by a solution containing $0.822 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute in $300 \mathrm{~mL}$ of water at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. (Molar mass of solute $ = 340 {\text{g}} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}},{\text{ R}} = 0.0821\,{\text{L atm}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} {{\text{K}}^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=0.822 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{~V}=300 \mathrm{~mL}=0.3 \mathrm{~L}$,$\mathrm{T}=300 \mathrm{~K}$,
${{\text{M}}_2} = 340 {\text{g }} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}},{\text{R}} = 0.0821\,{\text{L atm }}{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\pi=$ ?
$\pi=\mathrm{CRT}$
$\pi=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}}$
$=\dfrac{0.822 \mathrm{~g} \times 0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}{340 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 0.3 \mathrm{~L}}$
$=0.2 \mathrm{~atm}$

$ \Rightarrow {\rm{h = 1221}}\,\,{\rm{cm}}$

question: A 5.25% solution of a substance is isotonic with a 1.5% solution of urea $\left( {{\rm{molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass = 60g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$ in the same solvent. explanation:
Isotonic solutions have same osmotic pressure ${{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{RT,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{RT}}$

${\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.5}}}}{{{\rm{60}}}}}}{{\rm{V}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\frac{{{\rm{5}}{\rm{.25}}}}{{\rm{M}}}}}{{\rm{V}}}$
[Where M = Molecular Weight of the substance]

question:
Which of the following statements are true about osmotic pressure?
I. Flow of solvent from solution across a semipermeable membrane can be stopped, if some extra pressure is applied on solution.
II. It is the pressure that stops the flow of solvent towards solution.
III. It is the pressure that allows flow of solvent across the semipermeable membrane.
explanation: The flow of solvent from its side to solution across a semipermeable membrane can be stopped, if some extra pressure is applied on the solution. This pressure that just stops the flow of solvent is called osmotic pressure of the solution.
subject: Chemistry

question: The following solutions were prepared by dissolving 10 g of glucose $\left( {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{6}}}} \right)$ in 250 Ml of water $\left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right)$, 10 g of urea $\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}} \right)$ in 250 mL of water $\left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right)$ and 10 g of sucrose $\left( {{{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{22}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{{\rm{11}}}}} \right)$ in 250 mL of water $\left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)$. The right option for the decreasing order of osmotic pressure of these solutions is :
explanation:
${\rm{\pi = iCRT}}$
${\rm{ = i}}\frac{{\left[ {{\rm{Mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute}}} \right]}}{{\left[ {{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solute \times Volume}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{solution}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{in}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L}}} \right]}}{\rm{ \times RT}}$
C = Concentration of solution
i = 1 for all non-electrolyte solute
${\rm{Osmatic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{pressure}} \propto \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}$
${\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass:Glucose}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Urea}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Sucrose}}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{180}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{342}}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{1}}}} \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)$
${\rm{Order}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{osmatic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{pressure}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{:}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{3}}}$

question: The empirical formula of a non electrolyte is ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$. A solution containing 3 g of the compound exerts the same osmotic pressure as that of 0.05 M glucose solution. The molecular formula of the compound is
explanation:
For isotonic solution, $\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{x}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{x}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{W}}_{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{6}}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{12}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{6}}}}}$
$\frac{{\rm{3}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{x}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{9}}}{{{\rm{180}}}} \Rightarrow {{\rm{M}}_{\rm{x}}}{\rm{ = 60}}$
$\left[ {\because {\text{0}}{\text{.05M}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{6}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{12}}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{6}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{containing}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{9}}\,{\text{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{glucose}}} \right]$
Molecular weight of electrolyte = 60
Empirical formula ${\rm{ = C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
Empirical formula mass = 12 + 2 + 16 = 30
Molecular formula ${\rm{ = n \times }}$ empirical formula
${\rm{n = }}\frac{{{\rm{Molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{{{\rm{Emperical}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{formula}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{60}}}}{{{\rm{30}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}$
Molecular formula ${\rm{ = 2 \times C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O = }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is osmotic pressure of a semi molar solution at ${\rm{2}}{{\rm{7}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$?  (R = 0.082 ${\rm{L}}\,\,{\rm{atm}}\,\,{\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}\,{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$)
explanation:
Given,
Concentration (C) = semi -molar solution that
means concentration $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}$m of solution is half.
${\rm{T = 2}}{{\rm{7}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C = 300K}}$
${\rm{R = 0}}{\rm{.082}}$
we know that,
Osmotic pressure $\left( {\rm{\pi }} \right){\rm{ = CRT}}$
${\rm{\pi = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ \times 0}}{\rm{.082 \times 300 = 12}}{\rm{.3atm}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Osmotic pressure of the solution can be increased by
explanation: ${\rm{\pi  = CRT = }}\frac{{\rm{n}}}{{\rm{V}}}{\rm{RT;\pi }} \propto {\rm{T}}$

question: 18 gram glucose (Molar mass = 180) is dissolved in 100 ml of water at 300 K. If R = 3 ${\rm{0}}{\rm{.0821}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L - atm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ what is the osmotic pressure of solution?
explanation:
The various quantities known to us are as follows:
${\rm{R = 0}}{\rm{.0821L - atm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
${{\rm{W}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 18}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{gram}}$
${\rm{Molar}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}\left( {{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right){\rm{ = 180}}$
${\rm{T = 300}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{K}}$
${\rm{V = 100}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mL}}$
To calculate the osmotic pressure of solution, we use the following formula,
${\rm{\pi = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{RT}}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{V}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{18 \times 0}}{\rm{.0821 \times 300 \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{180 \times 100}}}}$
${\rm{ = 24}}{\rm{.63}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atm}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The osmotic pressure of solution containing 34.2 g of cane sugar (molar mass ${\rm{ = 342}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$) in 1L of solution at ${\rm{2}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$  is (Given ${\rm{R = 0}}{\rm{.082}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{L}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$)
explanation:
Osmotic pressure, ${\rm{\pi = CRT}}$
where, C = concentration of solution.
Given, w = 34.3 g, V = 1 L, M ${\rm{ = 342}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$
${\rm{T = 2}}{{\rm{0}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C, = 20 + 273 = 29}}{{\rm{3}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{C}}$
$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi = }}\frac{{\rm{W}}}{{{\rm{MV}}}}{\rm{RT = }}\frac{{{\rm{34}}{\rm{.2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}}}{{{\rm{342}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}}}$
${\rm{ \times }}\frac{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.082L}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{K}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ \times 293k}}}}{{{\rm{1L}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.40}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{atm}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A 6% solution of urea is isotonic with
explanation:
When a solution of urea (6%) is isotonic with a solution of glucose,
${{\rm{\pi }}_{{\rm{glucose}}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{\pi }}_{{\rm{urea}}}} \Rightarrow {{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{glucose}}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{C}}_{{\rm{urea}}}}$
$\therefore \left( {\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}}}{{{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ \times 1000}}}}{{{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ \times 100}}}}} \right)$
${\rm{where,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ = mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{glucose = x}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
${{\rm{m}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ = molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{glucose = 180}}$
${{\rm{w}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ = mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{urea = 6}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}$
${{\rm{m}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ = molecular}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{urea = 60}}$
$\therefore \frac{{{\rm{x \times 100}}}}{{{\rm{180 \times 100}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{6 \times 1000}}}}{{{\rm{60 \times 100}}}} \Rightarrow {\rm{x = 18g}}$
Thus, 18 g of glucose are present in 100 mL of solution.
In other words, 1 M solution of glucose (18 g in 100 mL) is isotonic with 6% solution of urea.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is not an industrial or biological importance of osmosis?

options.text: Movement of water from soil into plant roots and upper portion of plant
Salting of meat to prevent bacterial action
Reverse osmosis for desalination of sea water
Filling of ink in a fountain pen

subject: Chemistry

question: If M, W and V represent molar mass of solute, the mass of solute and volume of solution in litres respectively, which among following equations is true?
explanation:
We have the relation between osmotic
pressure and molecular mass as,
${\rm{\pi = CRT}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{;}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{where,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi = osmotic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{pressure}}$
${\rm{C = concentration}}$
${\rm{\pi = }}\frac{{\rm{n}}}{{\rm{V}}}{\rm{RT}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi = }}\frac{{\frac{{\rm{W}}}{{\rm{M}}}}}{{\rm{V}}}{\rm{ \times RT}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{\rm{TWR}}}}{{{\rm{MV}}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: A solution has an osmotic pressure of ‘x’ kPa at 300 K having 1 mole of solute in ${\rm{10}}{\rm{.5}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of solution. If it’s osmotic pressure is reduced to $\left( {\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}} \right){\rm{th}}$ of it’s initial value, what is the new volume of solution?
explanation:
The given values are
${{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = 'x' kPa,}}{{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{x}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{kPa,}}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = 300K,}}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = 1mole}}$
Using formula,
${{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{V = }}{{\rm{x}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{RT}}$
${\rm{x \times 10}}{\rm{.5 = 1 \times 8}}{\rm{.314 \times 300}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{x = }}\frac{{{\rm{8}}{\rm{.314 \times 300}}}}{{{\rm{10}}{\rm{.5}}}}$
If osmotic pressure is reduced $\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{th}}$ then,
value ${{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{x}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{kPa}}$
${{\rm{\pi }}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{V = nRT}}$
$\frac{{\rm{X}}}{{{\rm{10}}}}{\rm{ \times V = n \times R \times T}}$
$\frac{{{\rm{8}}{\rm{.314 \times 300}}}}{{{\rm{10}}{\rm{.5 \times 10}}}}{\rm{ \times V = 1 \times 8}}{\rm{.314 \times 300}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{V = 10}}{\rm{.5 \times 10 = 105}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following sets of solutions of urea $\left( {{\rm{mol}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{60}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$ and sucrose $\left( {{\rm{mol}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mass}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{342}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}} \right)$  is isotonic?
subject: Chemistry

question:
After adding non-volatile solute freezing point of water decreases to $-0.186^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}$ if ${{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} = 1.86\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$ and
${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} = 0.521\,{\text{K}}\,{\text{kg}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
explanation:
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}} \times {\text{m}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{m}} = \frac{{\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}}}{{{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}}} = \frac{{0.186}}{{1.86}} = 0.1\,{\text{m}}$
$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}} = {{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \times {\text{m}} = 0.521 \times 0.1 = 0.0521\,{\text{K}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: On mixing urea, the boiling point of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is changed to $100.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Calculate the freezing point of the solution, if   $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of water is $1.87 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
explanation:
$\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.5}{\Delta \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\dfrac{0.52}{1.87}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.8$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0-1.8=-1.8^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: At $300 \mathrm{~K}, 0.06 \mathrm{~kg}$ of an organic solute is dissolved in 1 kg water. The vapour pressure of solution at 300 K is 3.768 kPa.   If vapour pressure of water at that temperature is 3.78 kPa,   what is the molar mass of the organic solute (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) ?   (Assume the solution in dilute)
explanation:
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^0} - {\rm{p}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}^0}}} = \frac{{{{\rm{w}}_2} \times {{\rm{M}}_1}}}{{{{\rm{M}}_2} \times {{\rm{w}}_1}}}$
$\mathrm{M}_{2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}_{2} \times \mathrm{M}_{1} \times \mathrm{p}^{0}}{\left(\mathrm{p}^{0}-\mathrm{p}\right) \times \mathrm{w}_{1}}$
$\mathrm{w}_{2}=0.06 \mathrm{~kg}=60 \mathrm{~g}$
$\mathrm{m}_{1}=18 \mathrm{~g} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{p}^{0}=3.78 \mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{Pa}$
$\mathrm{p}=3.768 \mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{Pa}$
$\mathrm{w}_{1}=1 \mathrm{Kg}=1000 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}_{2}=\dfrac{60 \times 18 \times 3.78}{(3.78-3.768) \times 1000}$
$=\dfrac{4.0824}{0.012}=340.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
subject: Chemistry

question: At  ${\rm{T}}({\rm{K}}),0.1$  moles of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 0.9 moles of a volatile solvent. The vapour pressure of pure solvent is 0.9 bar.   What is the vapour pressure (in bar) of solution?
explanation:
No. of moles of solvent, ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{A}}} = 0.1$ mole
No. of moles of solvent, $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}=0.9$ mole
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\dfrac{0.1}{0.9+0.1}=0.1$
from Raoults Law,
$\dfrac{\mathrm{p}^{0}-\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}^{0}}=\mathrm{X}_{\text {solute }}$
$\dfrac{0.9-\mathrm{p}}{0.9}=0.1$
$0.9-\mathrm{p}=0.09$
$\mathrm{p}=0.9-0.9=0.81$ bar
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole fraction of the solute in the solution.
Relative lowering of vapour pressure can be used to calculate the molar mass of the solute.
explanation: The relative lowering of vapour pressure is indeed equal to the mole  fraction of a nonvolatile solute and this property can be used to calculate the molar mass of the solute. Correct option is (1)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Solutions of glucose in water are non-ideal solutions.
The boiling point of a solution is always lower than the boiling point of the pure solvent.
explanation: Solutions of glucose in water are typically non-ideal. However, the boiling point of a solution is generally higher, not lower, than the boiling point of the pure solvent. This is due to the solution's vapour pressure being lower than that of the pure solvent, necessitating a higher temperature to reach the boiling point. Correct option is (2)

question:

The vapour pressure of a solution with a non-volatile solute is equal to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent.
The presence of a non-volatile solute in a solvent decreases the solution's vapour pressure compared to the pure solvent.
explanation: The vapour pressure of a solution with a non-volatile solute is lower than that of the pure solvent. This occurs because the solute particles occupy space on the surface of the solution, reducing the number of solvent molecules that can escape into the vapour phase. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.

explanation: Statement  A   is true as per Raoult's law; Statement B is false because the presence of a non-volatile solute lowers the vapour pressure of a solution compared to the pure solvent. Correct option is (2)

All solutions of solids in liquids increases the vapour pressure after dissolution.

explanation: When a non-volatile solute is dissolved in a solvent, the vapour pressure of the resulting solution is indeed lower than that of the pure solvent because the solute particles occupy space at the surface that would otherwise be available for solvent molecules to evaporate, thus reducing the vapour pressure . Correct option is (3)

question: At $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the vapour pressure of pure benzene is 268 torr. The number of moles of non-volatile solute per mole of benzene required to prepare a solution having a vapour pressure of 167 torr at the same temperature is (molar mass of benzene $=78 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Raoult's Law
$\frac{{{\text{p}}^\circ - {{\text{p}}_{\text{s}}}}}{{{\text{p}}{^\circ _{\text{s}}}}} = \frac{{{{\text{n}}_{{\text{solute }}}}}}{{{{\text{n}}_{{\text{total }}}}}}$
$\dfrac{268-167}{167}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{\text {solute }}}{1}$
$\mathrm{n}_{\text {solute }}=0.605$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure of pure water is 23 mmHg . The vapour pressure of an aqueous solution, which contains 10 mass percent of solute 'A' having molecular weight 50 is
explanation:
$\frac{{{\text{p}}^\circ - {{\text{p}}_{\text{s}}}}}{{{\text{p}}^\circ }} = \frac{{\text{w}}}{{\text{m}}} \times \frac{{\text{M}}}{{\text{W}}}\quad \quad $ (Raoult's law)
$\dfrac{23-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}}{23}=\dfrac{10}{50} \times \dfrac{18}{90}$
$\dfrac{23-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}}{23}=0.04$
$23-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}=23 \times 0.04=0.92$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}=23-0.92=22.08 \mathrm{~mm}$
(or) $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}=\dfrac{22.08}{760}=0.0289 \mathrm{~atm}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Relative lowering of vapour pressure of a dilute solution is 0.5 . What is the mole  fraction of the non-volatile solute?
explanation:
Raoult's law,
RLVP $=\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}}=\mathrm{X}_{\text {solute }}=0.5$ (given)
subject: Chemistry

question: How many grams of glucose are required to prepare an aqueous solution of glucose having a vapour pressure of 23.324 mm Hg at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 100 g of water? The vapour pressure of pure water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 23.8 mm Hg.  (Molar mass of glucose $=180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
explanation:
Raoult's law, $\frac{{{\text{p}}^\circ - {{\text{p}}_{\text{s}}}}}{{{{\text{p}}_{\text{s}}}}} = \frac{{\text{w}}}{{\text{m}}} \times \frac{{\text{M}}}{{\text{w}}}$
$\mathrm{p}^{\circ}=23.8, \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}=23.324$
$\mathrm{w}=?, \mathrm{~m}=180 \mathrm{~g} . \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{23.8-23.324}{23.324}=\dfrac{\mathrm{w}}{180} \times \dfrac{18}{100}$
$\mathrm{w}=20.4$ gram .
subject: Chemistry

question: When a non-volatile solute is added to the solvent, the vapour pressure of the solvent decreases by 10 mm of Hg.   The mole  fraction of solute in the solution is 0.2. What would be the mole   fraction of the solvent if decrease in vapour pressure is 20 mm of Hg.
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}^{\circ}-\mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{X}_{\text {Solute }}$
$10 \propto 0.2$
$\therefore 20 \propto 0.4$
$\therefore \mathrm{X}_{\text {Solvent }}=1-\mathrm{X}_{\text {Solute }}=1-0.4=0.6$
subject: Chemistry

question: At $88^\circ {\text{C}}$, benzene has a vapour pressure of 900 torr and toluene has a vapour pressure of 360 torr. Assuming that benzene and toluene form an ideal solution, calculate the mole fraction of benzene in a mixture if the total pressure is 500 torr at $88^\circ {\text{C}}$.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} x_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0} x_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} x_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0}\left(1-x_{\mathrm{A}}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 500=900 x_{\mathrm{A}}+360\left(1-x_{\mathrm{A}}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore \quad 500=900 x_{\mathrm{A}}+360-360 x_{\mathrm{A}}}}$
${\mathrm{\therefore 140=540 x_{\mathrm{A}}}}$
Therefore, ${\mathrm{x_{\mathrm{A}}=0.26}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Vapour pressure of a pure solvent is 100 mm Hg . When certain amount of nonvolatile, non-electrolyte solute added it, its vapour pressure is lowered by 10 mm Hg . The value of mole fraction of solute will be ____.
explanation: ${\mathrm{x_{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0}-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0}}=\dfrac{100-90}{100}=0.1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: At a certain temperature vapour pressure of pure water is $3000 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2} .5 \mathrm{~g}$ of non-electrolyte and non-volatile solute is added to 100 g of water. Vapour pressure of the solution is $2985\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - 2}}$. Assume that it is a dilute solution, find the molar mass of the solute.
explanation:
Vapour pressure of pure water,
${\rm{P}}_{{\rm{water}}}^{\rm{o}} = 3000{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{N}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - 2}}$
Weight of water=100 g
Weight of non-volatile solute =5 g
Vapour pressure of solution,${\rm{P}} = 2985{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - 2}}$
Molar mass of solute =M (say)
${\rm{We}}\,{\rm{have, }}\frac{{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{water}}}^{\rm{^\circ }} - {\rm{P}}}}{{{\rm{P}}_{{\rm{water}}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}}\,{\rm{ = }}\,{\rm{x}}{{\mkern 1mu} _{{\rm{(non}}{\kern 1pt} {\rm{ - }}{\kern 1pt} {\rm{volatile}}{\kern 1pt} \,{\rm{solute)}}}}$
$\frac{{3000 - 2985}}{{3000}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{\rm{M}}}}}{{\frac{5}{{\rm{M}}} + \frac{{100}}{{18}}}}$
As number of moles of non-volatile solute is very less it can be neglected from denominator.
$\frac{{3000 - 2985}}{{3000}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{{\rm{M}}}}}{{\frac{{100}}{{18}}}}$
$5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times \frac{{100}}{{18}} = \frac{5}{{\rm{M}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{M}} = 180\,{\rm{g\,mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Vapour pressure of chloroform $\left(\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\right)$ and dichloromethane $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are200 mm Hg and 41.5 mm Hg respectively. Vapour pressure of the solution obtained by mixing 25.5 g of ${\text{CHC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}$ and 40 g of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$ at the same temperature will be : (Molecular mass of ${\rm{CHC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}$ = 119.5 u and molecular mass of ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$ = 85 u).
subject: Chemistry

question:
The degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ in dilute aq. solution containing $7.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of salt per
$100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $70 \%$. If vapour pressure of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $760 \mathrm{~mm} \,\mathrm{Hg}$. The vapour pressure of solution is

question: The vapour pressure lowering caused by the addition of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of sucrose (molecular mass $=342$) to $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water, if the vapour pressure of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $23.8 \mathrm{~mm} \,\mathrm{Hg}$ is
explanation:
Number of moles of $\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}$
$ = \frac{{100}}{{342}}$
$ = 0.292\,{\rm{mol}}$
Number of moles of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\dfrac{1000}{18}$
$=55.5 \mathrm{~mol}$
$(\because$ Molecular mass of sucrose $=342$ ),
(Molecular mass of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=18$ )
Vapour pressure of pure water ${\rm{p}}^\circ = 23.8\,{\rm{mm\,Hg}}$
According to Raoult's law,$\frac{{{\rm{\Delta p}}}}{{{\rm{p}}^\circ }} = \frac{{\rm{n}}}{{{\rm{n}} + {\rm{N}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\rm{\Delta p}}}}{{{\rm{23}}{\rm{.8}}}} = \frac{{0.292}}{{0.292 + 55.5}}$
${\rm{\Delta p}} = \frac{{23.8 \times 0.292}}{{55.79}} = 0.125\,{\rm{mm\,Hg}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Lowering of vapour pressure of an aqueous solution of a non-volatile, non-electrolyte $1 \mathrm{~m}$ aqueous solution at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
explanation:
${\rm{p}}^\circ = 760$ torr,$1 \mathrm{~m}$ aqueous solution contains 1 mole of solute in $1000 \mathrm{~g}(55.5 \mathrm{~mol})$ of water.
${{\rm{\chi }}_{\rm{B}}} = \frac{1}{{1 + 55.5}} = \frac{1}{{56.5}}$
$\frac{{{\rm{\Delta p}}}}{{{\rm{p}}^\circ }} = {{\rm{\chi }}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{,\Delta p}} = {\rm{p}}^\circ {{\rm{\chi }}_{\rm{B}}}$
$ = \frac{{760 \times 1}}{{56.5}} = 13.45{\rm{torr}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure curves of the same solute in the same solvent are shown. The curves are parallel to each other and do not intersect. The concentrations of solutions are in order of
explanation: Higher the concentration, lower is vapour pressure higher will be the boiling point.
subject: Chemistry

question: The solubility of a specific non-volatile salt is 4 g in 100 g of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If $2.0 \mathrm{~g}, 4.0 \mathrm{~g}$ and 6.0 g of the salt is added to 100 g of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, in system $\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}$ and Z , respectively. The vapour pressure would be in the order
explanation:
Solubility of salt is 4 g in $100\,{\rm{g}}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$.
So, if salt is less than 4 g , its vapour pressure will be high but on increasing solute to 4 g vapour pressure will decrease to maximum. On further adding solute (more than 4 g ), it won't affect the V.P. as now solute won't be a part of solution and will remain separated as ppt.
So, V.P. order: $\mathrm{X}>\mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{Z}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation: Generally solid is non-volatile. So, vapour pressure will be directly propotional to the mole fraction of solvent.

question:
What weight of glucose must be dissolved in 100 g of water to lower the vapour pressure by 0.20 mm Hg ? (Assume dilute solution is being formed.)
(Given : Vapour pressure of pure water is 54.2 mm Hg at room temperature. Molar mass of glucose is $180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. )
explanation:
Let weight of glucose be ${\rm{ }}{{\rm{W}}_{{\rm{B }}}}{\rm{g}}{\rm{.}}$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{B}}$
or $\dfrac{0.2}{54.2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{B} \times \mathrm{M}_{A}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}$ or $\quad \dfrac{0.2}{54.2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}} \times 18}{180 \times 100}$
or $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}=\dfrac{0.2 \times 180 \times 100}{54.2 \times 18}=3.69 \mathrm{~g}$
Thus, 3.69 g of glucose must be dissolved.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: If a liquid solute, more volatile than the solvent, is added to the solvent, the vapour pressure of the solution may increase i.e., $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}>\mathrm{p}^{\circ}$.


Reason: In the presence of a more volatile liquid solute, only the solute will form the vapours and solvent will not.
explanation: In a solution, as it evaporates, the vapor phase becomes enriched with the more volatile component, characterized by a higher vapor pressure or lower boiling point, due to its greater tendency of transition from the liquid to the gas phase. But along with solute molecules, solvent molecules also evaporates. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: The vapour pressure of a liquid decreases if some non-volatile solute is dissolved in it.


Reason: The relative lowering of vapour pressure of a solution containing a non-volatile solute is equal to the mole fraction of the solute in the solution.
explanation:
The introduction of a non-volatile solute reduces the vapor pressure of a liquid solution by displacing solvent molecules at the surface, hindering their escape into the vapor phase.
${\text{RLVP}},{\text{ }}\frac{{\Delta {{\text{P}}_1}}}{{{\text{P}}_1^0}} = {\chi _2}\,({\text{Raoult's}}\,{\text{law}})$
Relative lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole dfraction of solute. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure of pure solvent is $0.8 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ at a particular temperature. On addition of a non-volatile solute ' $\mathrm{A}$ ', the vapour pressure of solution becomes $0.6 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$. The mole dfraction of component ' $\mathrm{A}$ ' is:
explanation:
Acc. to Raoult's law,
$\dfrac{\mathrm{P}^{0}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{P}^{0}}=\mathrm{x}_{\text {solute }} \Rightarrow \dfrac{0.8-0.6}{0.8}=\mathrm{x}_{\text {solute }} \Rightarrow 0.25$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is vapour pressure of a solution containing $0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of non-volatile solute dissolved in $16.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of water? $\left(\mathrm{p}_{1}^{0}=32 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}\right)$
subject: Chemistry

question: Vapour pressure of solution and of pure solvent are $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}$ respectively. If $\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}$ is 0.15 , find the mole dfraction of solute.
explanation:
$\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}-\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\mathrm{x}_{2}$ (mole dfraction of solute)
$\therefore \dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}-\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\mathrm{x}_{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}_{2}=1-0.15=0.85$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is vapour pressure of solution containing 0.1 mole solute dissolved in $1.8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ? $\left( {{\text{P}}_1^0 = 24\,{\text{mm Hg}}} \right)$
explanation:
$\mathop {{{\text{n}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{solute}}} {\text{ = 0}}{\text{.1}}\,\,{\text{mol,}}\,\,{\text{P}}_{\text{1}}^{\text{0}}{\text{ = 24}}\,\,{\text{mm}}\,\,{\text{Hg}}$
${{\text{W}}_1} = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 2}} {\text{kg }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} = 18 {\text{g}},{{\text{M}}_1} = 18 {\text{g}} {\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \underset{\text { (solvent) }}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}=\dfrac{18 \mathrm{~g}}{18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Now, $\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}-\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}$ (mole dfraction of solute)
$\therefore \frac{{{\text{24}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{mm Hg - }}{{\text{P}}_{\text{1}}}}}{{{\text{24}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{mm Hg}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.1}}}}{{{\text{1 + 0}}{\text{.1}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.1}}}}{{{\text{1}}{\text{.1}}}}{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.09}}$
$\therefore 24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} - {{\text{P}}_1} = 2.16\,\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
$\therefore {{\text{P}}_1} = 24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} - 2.16\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} = 21.84\,\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is vapour pressure of a solution containing $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of a non - volatile solute in $36 \mathrm{~g}$ of water? $\left(\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}=400 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}\right)$
explanation:
$\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}=400 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{n}_{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$, (solute)
$\mathrm{n}_{1}=\dfrac{36}{18}=2 \mathrm{~mol}$ (solvent)
$\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}-\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{400-\mathrm{P}_{1}}{400}=\dfrac{1}{3}$
$\therefore 400-\mathrm{P}_{1}=133.33$
$\therefore 400-133.33=\mathrm{P}_{1}$
$\therefore \mathrm{P}_{1}=266.67 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
What is vapour pressure of solution containing $1.8 \mathrm{~g}$ glucose in $16.2 \mathrm{~g}$ water?
$\left(\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}=24 \mathrm{mmHg}\right.$ and Molar mass of glucose $=180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
$\mathrm{W}_{2}=1.8 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{M}_{2}=180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{~W}_{1}=16.2 \mathrm{~g}$,
${{\text{M}}_1} = 18\,{\text{g mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}},{\text{P}}_1^0 = 24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}},{{\text{P}}_1} = ?$
$\dfrac{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}-\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{W}_{2} \mathrm{M}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~W}_{1}}$
$\therefore \frac{{24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} - {{\text{P}}_1}}}{{24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}}}} = \frac{{1.8\,{\text{g}} \times 18\,{\text{g}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}}}{{180\,{\text{g}}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}} \times 16.2\,{\text{g}}}}$
$\therefore 24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} - {{\text{P}}_1} = 24{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{mm Hg}}(0.0111)$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{P}}_1} = 24\,\,{\text{mm Hg}} - 0.2664\,\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
$\therefore \quad {{\text{P}}_1} = 23.73\,\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure of a solvent decreases by $2.5 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$ by adding a solute. What is the mole dfraction of solute? (Vapour pressure of pure solvent is $250 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$ )
explanation:
$\Delta {\text{P}} = 2.5\,{\text{mm Hg}},{\text{P}}_1^0 = 250\,{\text{mm Hg}}$ According to Raoult's law,
${{\text{x}}_2} = \frac{{\Delta {\text{P}}}}{{{\text{P}}_1^0}} = \frac{{2.5}}{{250}} = 0.01$
subject: Chemistry

question: What is vapour pressure of a solution when 2 mol of a non - volatile solute is dissolved in $20 \mathrm{~mol}$ of water?  $\left( {{\text{P}}{^\circ _1} = 32 {\text{mm Hg}}} \right)$
explanation:
${\text{P}}_1^0 = 32\,\,{\text{mm Hg}},{{\text{n}}_1} = \mathop {20 {\text{mol}}}\limits_{{\text{ (solvent) }}} ,{{\text{n}}_2} = \mathop {2 {\text{mol}}}\limits_{{\text{ (solute) }}} $
$\therefore \mathrm{x}_{1}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}=\dfrac{20}{20+2}=0.909$
from Raoult's law,
${{\text{P}}_1} = {\text{P}}_1^0{{\text{x}}_1} = 32{\text{mm Hg}} \times 0.909$
$ = 29.1 {\text{mm Hg}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: If vapour pressure of pure solvent and solution are ${\text{240}}$ and ${\text{216\,}}\,\,{\text{mm}}\,\,{\text{Hg}}$ respectively then mole dfraction of solvent in solution is
explanation:
${{\text{P}}_1} = 216\, {\text{mm Hg}}$, ${\text{P}}_{\text{1}}^{\text{0}} = 240\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
$\Delta {\text{P}} = {\text{P}}_1^0 - {{\text{P}}_1} = 240 - 216 = 24\,{\text{mm Hg}}$
According to Raoult's law, $\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}^{0}}=\mathrm{x}_{2}$
$\therefore {{\text{x}}_2} = \frac{{24\,{\text{mm Hg}}}}{{240\,{\text{mm Hg}}}} = 0.1$
Hence, mole fraction of solvent,
$\mathrm{x}_{1}=1-\mathrm{x}_{2}=1-0.1=0.9$
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure of pure $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are 200 and 41.5 atm respectively. The weight of $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are respectively $11.9 \mathrm{~g}$ and $17 \mathrm{~g}$. The vapour pressure of solution will be
explanation:
Given,
Vapour pressure of pure $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\left[\mathrm{p}_{1}\right]$ and
$\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\left[\mathrm{p}_{2}\right]=200 \mathrm{~atm}$ and $41.5 \mathrm{~atm}$
respectively. Weight $\left(\mathrm{w}_{1}\right)$ of $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {{{\text{w}}_{\text{2}}}} \right){\text{ = 11}}{\text{.9 g}}$ and ${\text{17 g}}$ respectively.
Molar mass of $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}\left(\mathrm{M}_{1}\right)$ and
$\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{M}_{2}\right)=119.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $85 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
respectively. Thus, number of moles (n) of
$\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}=\dfrac{11.9}{119.5}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Number of moles of $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}=\dfrac{17}{85}=0.2 \mathrm{~mol}$
As we know, $\mathrm{p}_{\text {total }}=\mathrm{p}_{1} \chi_{1}+\mathrm{p}_{2} \chi_{2}$
$\chi_{1}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\dfrac{0.1}{0.3}=0.33$
$\chi_{2}=\dfrac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\dfrac{0.2}{0.3}=0.66$
$\therefore \mathrm{p}_{\text {total }}=(200 \times 0.33)+(41.5 \times 0.66)=94.33 \mathrm{~atm}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In an experiment, 1g of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 100g of acetone (mol. mass = 58) at 298K. The vapour pressure of the solution was found to be 192.5 mm Hg. The molecular weight of the solute is (vapour pressure of acetone =195 mm Hg)
subject: Chemistry

question: The vapour pressure of benzene at ${\rm{80^\circ C}}$ is lowered by 10 mm when 2.0 g of a non-volatile substance is dissolved in 78 g of benzene. The vapour pressure of pure benzene at 80°C is 750 mm. The molar mass of the non-volatile substance in the given dilute solution is
explanation:
If ${{\rm{P}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}$ is the vapour pressure of pure benzene and P the vapour pressure of solution, w, the weight of non-volatile substance of molecular mass ‘m’ and W, the weight of solvent benzene with molecular mass M,
$\frac{{{{\rm{P}}^{\rm{0}}}{\rm{ - P}}}}{{{{\rm{P}}^{\rm{0}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{w/m}}}}{{{\rm{W/M}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{wM}}}}{{{\rm{Wm}}}}$
Substituting the values
$\frac{{{\rm{10}}}}{{{\rm{750}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{2 \times 78}}}}{{{\rm{78 \times m}}}} \Rightarrow {\rm{m = 150}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.0133 = }}\frac{{{\rm{156}}}}{{{\rm{78 \times m}}}}$
${\rm{Or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{m}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{{{\rm{156}}}}{{{\rm{78 \times 0}}{\rm{.0133}}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{150}}{\rm{.37}} \sim {\rm{150}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Three solutions are prepared by adding ‘w’ gram of ‘A’ into 1 kg of water, ‘w’ gram of ‘B’ into another 1 kg of water and ‘w’ gram of ‘C’ in another 1 kg of water (A, B, C are non electrolyte). Dry air is passed from these solutions in sequence $\left( {A \to B \to C} \right)$. The loss in weight of solution A was found to be 2g, while solution B gained 0.5 g and solution C lost 1 g. Then the relation between molar masses of A, B and C are
explanation:
The loss in water should be proportional to vapor pressure above that solution :
${\rm{So,}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{A}}}}} \propto {\rm{2g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{B}}}}} \propto {\rm{1}}{\rm{.5g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{P}}_{{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{C}}}}} \propto {\rm{2}}{\rm{.5g}}$
So, maximum vapor pressure is above C solution; hence it is having minimum lowering and hence minimum mole fraction (hence minimum number of moles of solute). So, maximum molar mass of a substance.

question: 3 g of urea is dossolved in 45 g of ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$. The relative lowering in vapour pressure is
explanation:
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is given by
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}$
For a dilute solution, ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}$ can be neglected, then
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{3/60}}}}{{{\rm{45/18}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.02mm}}$
(where, ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{2}}}$ for urea and ${{\rm{n}}_{\rm{1}}}$ for water)
subject: Chemistry

question: The mass of glucose that should be dissolved in 50 g of water in order to produce the same lowering of vapour pressure as is produced by dissolving 1 g of urea in the same quantity of water is
explanation:
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{2}}}}}{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{1}}}}}$
To produce same lowering of vapour pressure
$\left( {\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}} \right)$
term will be same for both the cases
(solvent is same).
$\therefore \frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ \times 18}}}}{{{\rm{50 \times 180}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ \times 18}}}}{{{\rm{50 \times 60}}}}$
$\left( {{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ = weight}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{glucose,}}\,\,{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{u}}}{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{weight}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{urea}}} \right)$
${\rm{or,}}\,\,\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ \times 18}}}}{{{\rm{50 \times 180}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1 \times 18}}}}{{{\rm{50 \times 60}}}}{\rm{or,}}\,\,{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{g}}}{\rm{ = 3}}$

${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2 = }}\frac{{{\rm{100mm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Hg}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{A}}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{p}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10}}}}{{{\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}}}{\rm{mm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Hg = 50mm}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Hg}}$

question: Relative lowering of vapour pressure of a dilute solution of glucose dissolved in 1 kg of water is 0.002. The molality of the solution is

question: The mass of a non-volatile solute of molar mass ${\rm{40g}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}$ that should be dissolved in 114 g of octane to lower its vapour pressure by 20% is
explanation:
According to Raoult’s law,
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{/}}{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{B}}}}}{{{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{B}}}}}}}$
where, ${{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{,}}{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{A}}} \to $ weight dissolved and molecular mass of solute respectively.
${{\rm{w}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{,}}{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{B}}} \to $ weight dissolved and molecular mass of solvent respectively.
${{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}} \to $ vapour pressure of pure solvent
${{\rm{p}}_{\rm{s}}} \to $ vapour pressure of solution.
$\frac{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{P}}_{\rm{s}}}}}{{{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{^\circ }}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{20}}}}{{{\rm{100}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.2}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2 = }}\frac{{\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}}}{{\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}{\rm{ + }}\frac{{{\rm{114}}}}{{{\rm{114}}}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{/40}}}}{{\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ + 40}}}}{{{\rm{40}}}}}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}\frac{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{{{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ + 40}}}}$
${\rm{0}}{\rm{.2}}{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ + 8 = }}{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}$
${\rm{8 = }}{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ - 0}}{\rm{.2}}{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.8}}{{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{w}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = 10g}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Dry air contains $79 \% \mathrm{~N}_{2}$ and $21 \% \mathrm{O}_{2}$. At  $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$, if Henry's law constants for  $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ in water are  $8.57 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~atm}$  and   $4.56 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~atm}$, the ratio of mole  fractions of   $\mathrm{N}_{2}$  and    $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ dissolved in water at 1 atm is
explanation:
Total pressure of air over water $=1 \mathrm{~atm}$. Partial pressure of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ are
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\dfrac{1 \times 79}{100}=0.79 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\dfrac{1 \times 21}{100}=0.21 \mathrm{~atm}$
Applying Henry's law $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}=\dfrac{0.79}{8.57 \times 10^{4}}=9.25 \times 10^{-6}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\dfrac{0.21}{4.56 \times 10^{4}}=4.60 \times 10^{-6}$
Ratio of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}=$
$9.25 \times 10^{-6}: 4.60 \times 10^{-6}=2: 1$.
subject: Chemistry
